Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The commenter opposes loosening certification rules for hospital AI systems, arguing that transparency and safety testing requirements are essential to protect patients like their stroke-affected mother, and that hospitals need clear accountabilit...
Comment:
My mother had a stroke three years ago and I've been managing her care since then. When she switched clinics, her records didn't follow her for two weeks. The doctors kept asking me the same questions over and over—her medications, her history, everything I'd already told them multiple times. I spent those two weeks driving back and forth trying to track down copies myself instead of being with her.

Here's what worries me about loosening the rules around certification: hospitals are going to buy new AI tools and systems without really knowing what they're designed for or who they work best with. When things change like that, someone needs to be checking whether the tool actually works for patients like my mother. Right now I'm already exhausted managing her appointments and medications. The last thing I need is to also have to worry whether the hospital's new system has actually been tested for safety or if they just grabbed it because it was cheap.

I understand companies want less red tape, but transparency requirements exist because when things go wrong, it's the patient and family who suffer. My mother deserves care decisions based on tools that have been properly checked, not guesses. Hospitals need clear information about what they're buying. That's not burden—that's basic responsibility. Please keep those standards in place.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The commenter supports streamlining health record sharing but opposes removing AI transparency requirements for medical tools, arguing that doctors need detailed information about how AI systems work, their testing populations, and failure modes t...
Comment:
My daughter's diabetes management got turned upside down last year when our insurance changed and her new doctor couldn't access her blood sugar records from the old clinic. We literally had to carry a folder of printed papers back and forth like it was the 1990s. So when I heard they're working on making health records easier to share, I thought finally—something that actually helps patients.

But then I found out they're talking about removing the requirements that make doctors understand how the AI tools they're using actually work. That terrifies me. How is my daughter's endocrinologist supposed to catch a mistake if the AI recommends the wrong insulin dosage and nobody even knows what populations the system was tested on or what its failures look like? It's one thing to streamline paperwork. It's completely different to hide information from the people making medical decisions.

When you remove transparency requirements for AI systems, you're not cutting red tape—you're cutting the information doctors need to protect their patients. My doctor should know what that algorithm was trained on, whether it works the same for kids as adults, what it can't do well. Calling this "deregulation" when it's really just hiding information behind closed doors doesn't make it innovation. It makes it dangerous.

If the goal is actually helping patients, then keep the rules that force companies to tell doctors the truth about how these systems work. Everything else should come second to that.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: Loosening certification requirements for health IT systems, particularly those using AI, risks patient safety by eliminating transparency requirements that allow doctors to understand how these systems work and make informed decisions about their ...
Comment:
My daughter had to switch doctors last year when we moved, and it took three months just to get her medical records transferred. Her new doctor said the old clinic's system couldn't talk to theirs. She ended up getting duplicate tests and her medications got confused in the shuffle. It was frustrating to watch that happen when moving her information should have been straightforward.

I get that companies want to move faster and cut through red tape. But I work construction, and we don't skip safety inspections to save time. You inspect the foundation before you build the house. Same thing here. When you're talking about removing certification requirements for health IT systems, especially ones that use AI to make decisions about patient care, you're talking about skipping the inspection.

What bothers me is the "black box" problem. A doctor should be able to look under the hood and understand how an AI system was built, what data trained it, where it might fail. Right now if certification gets loosened, doctors won't have that information. How are they supposed to trust a system they can't see into? Model cards and detailed disclosures aren't bureaucratic waste—they're blueprints. I wouldn't hire a contractor who refused to show me his plans, and hospitals shouldn't have to use software they can't understand.

Cutting corners to speed up innovation sounds good until someone's health data gets exposed or an algorithm makes the wrong call because nobody actually knew how it worked. I'm all for progress, but not when it means risking my family's safety.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The Coalition for Health AI opposes removing AI model card transparency requirements for healthcare systems, arguing that the absence of outcome studies demonstrating their value should trigger regulatory caution rather than deregulation, particul...
Comment:
Re: Request for Information Related to the Health Technology Ecosystem – Deregulatory Actions to Unleash Prosperity

The Coalition for Health AI (CHAI) appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory framework. We represent stakeholders across healthcare institutions, AI developers, and patient advocates who share a commitment to innovation. However, we have significant concerns about removing AI model card transparency requirements during a period when healthcare AI adoption is accelerating faster than our ability to study its effects.

Last spring, my sister was diagnosed with a rare autoimmune condition. We spent months moving her records between specialists, and what struck me wasn't just the inconvenience—it was that no one could tell us definitively what data existed, where it lived, or who was using it for what purpose. A billing company called about a test she'd already paid for. That experience taught me something: access without accountability creates a different kind of problem. We fixed the portability issue, but we created new questions about whether those moved records were being used safely, especially when algorithms started making decisions about what got flagged as "relevant" for the next provider.

The agency's analysis suggests that absent published outcome studies demonstrating AI model card transparency benefits, these requirements lack demonstrated value and should be removed. We respectfully disagree with this framing. The absence of robust longitudinal evidence does not establish absence of benefit; it reflects that detailed transparency requirements are too recent to have generated the studies themselves. This inverts the burden of proof in healthcare contexts where trust is foundational.

Consider the timeline: significant healthcare AI deployment is occurring now. Transparency requirements are nascent. We cannot wait for a decade of outcome studies before building safeguards into systems already making clinical decisions. The evidence vacuum should trigger caution, not deregulation by default.

We urge ONC to maintain detailed AI model card disclosure requirements, particularly for systems affecting clinical decision-making or patient safety. These transparency standards are preconditions for trust, not luxuries awaiting empirical vindication. We're happy to discuss implementation approaches that balance burden reduction with meaningful oversight.

David Thomas
Policy Director, Coalition for Health AI
North Dakota

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: Removing model card and disclosure requirements will worsen healthcare fragmentation by leaving hospitals unable to transparently evaluate and safely integrate AI tools across institutional boundaries; instead, regulators should maintain baseline ...
Organization Name: The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists
Comment:
Last year, my research team at the College tried to do something straightforward: integrate electronic health records across three hospital systems in Western Pennsylvania to track pregnancy outcomes and maternal safety trends. We couldn't do it. The systems were certified under different standards, and each hospital's legal and compliance teams were terrified of the liability exposure if they tried to force interoperability without explicit certification alignment. A pregnant patient I'm still thinking about slipped through the cracks because her prenatal labs from the first hospital never reached her OB at the second one. That's when it became clear to me that the fragmentation we're trying to solve isn't really about certification burden—it's about the fact that hospitals have no trustworthy, standardized way to understand what they're actually adopting when they buy a new AI tool or health IT system.

I want to be direct about what concerns me in this deregulation proposal. Removing model card requirements and disclosure standards will make that problem worse, not better. When procurement teams at hospital systems evaluate AI tools for clinical deployment—especially tools that cross institutional boundaries like maternal health risk stratification or diagnostic decision support—they need documented, standardized information about what the model does, how it was trained, and what populations it's been tested on. That's not bureaucratic overhead; that's decision-making infrastructure. Right now, hospitals can't confidently say yes to interoperability because they can't compare tools transparently. Remove the model card requirement and you don't reduce burden; you shift it entirely onto each hospital to independently reverse-engineer what they're buying.

The tension everyone's discussing is real: we do need to reduce unnecessary burden on developers. But the answer isn't weaker transparency standards. It's smarter certification criteria that require model cards and basic performance documentation while streamlining redundant institutional review. I'd recommend keeping DSI model card disclosure as a baseline requirement but exempting certain low-risk classes of tools from extended validation timelines. That way, developers get faster market access for tools with transparent documentation, and hospitals can actually integrate systems across care settings because they have the information they need to make it safe. That's how you get both innovation and interoperability.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: A mid-sized health IT vendor argues that AI model transparency requirements (including training data, intended use, and performance limitations) must remain rigorous and uniformly applied in certification revisions, as uneven removal of these requ...
Comment:
Thank you for the opportunity to comment on the proposed certification revisions.

Two years ago, our health IT division spent eighteen months and $300,000 to certify a single module. During that same period, a competitor launched functionally identical capability in six weeks. They had the cash to absorb regulatory uncertainty; we didn't. That gap troubles me now as we look at what's being proposed here, because streamlining certification unevenly—removing transparency requirements while keeping others intact—will create exactly the kind of uneven playing field that favors vendors who can weather chaos.

I want to be direct: I'm skeptical of arguments that removing AI model card disclosure requirements reduces unnecessary burden. What they actually reduce is visibility. When a larger vendor can deploy an AI-assisted diagnostic module without documenting its training data, intended use, or known performance gaps, and a smaller vendor can't, the smaller vendor loses the most important competitive advantage transparency offers—predictability. Right now, because we're mid-sized, we can't absorb the cost of waiting out regulatory rewrites. We need to know the rules and know they apply equally. Detailed model cards level that ground.

The harder question is what harm prevention looks like. I've watched health IT roll out features that seemed reasonable in the lab and caused real problems at scale—nothing catastrophic, but persistent enough that they lingered for months. With AI, the stakes are different. Waiting for documented patient harm before requiring transparency is ethically indefensible in this space. We can prevent problems by requiring upfront disclosure of what these models were built on, what populations they were validated against, and where they're likely to fail. That's not regulatory burden; it's basic accountability.

On your second question about maintenance of certification: I'd suggest decoupling AI transparency requirements from general certification lightening. Transparency standards should stay rigorous and uniform precisely because they protect competition. Everything else—the recertification cycles, the attestation documentation, the interoperability testing frequency—those are fair game for streamlining. But if you reduce transparency requirements unevenly or eliminate them while keeping traditional certification criteria, you've created a playing field where deep-pocketed vendors win by default because they can afford regulatory ambiguity and smaller vendors can't.

The concern I keep circling back to is this: if you make the rules predictable and uniform, everyone can plan. We can staff for it. We can build it into our development timeline. What we can't do—what I literally cannot do with our current resources—is compete in an environment where the rules change unpredictably and only vendors with massive reserves can absorb the cost of that change.

Please keep AI transparency requirements as part of any certification modernization. Make them non-negotiable and apply them equally. That's the only scenario where smaller vendors aren't squeezed out.

Respectfully,

Ashley King
Vice President, Regulatory Affairs
Verve, LLC
Mississippi

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The Dental Standards Institute opposes removing transparency and auditability certification criteria, arguing that the absence of published outcome studies does not justify dismantling safeguards that are operationally necessary for breach detecti...
Comment:
Submitted via regulations.gov

The Dental Standards Institute appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory actions. Our organization works with dental practices, insurers, and patient advocates across the Pacific Northwest, and we have a direct stake in how transparency and accountability standards evolve in healthcare IT.

I want to address the RFI's framing around certification criteria and the absence of published outcome studies on model card transparency. This framing concerns me because it conflates two separate claims in a way that doesn't hold up to scrutiny. Yes, there are few peer-reviewed studies measuring the downstream impact of AI model transparency requirements. But the absence of those studies does not mean transparency lacks real-world benefit. These transparency standards are still new—we're talking about requirements that are only a few years old at most. We shouldn't expect a robust literature base to exist yet. The agency seems to be using that gap in evidence to justify removing safeguards that are operationally necessary right now.

Last year, a regional dental network experienced a significant breach. Patient records, including my daughter's orthodontic history and insurance details, were exposed for nearly two months before anyone detected it. The incident report revealed the practice had deliberately disabled interoperability alerts to streamline workflows. There was no audit trail. No one knew when records were accessed or who had looked at them. The absence of transparency requirements at that practice didn't make operations more efficient in any way that mattered—it just made it impossible to detect what was happening until damage was done.

This is the operational necessity I'm talking about. Detailed disclosures of model behavior, training data, validation processes—these aren't academic exercises. They are breach detection mechanisms. They create accountability. Real incidents of data mishandling demonstrate that these safeguards prevent harm, even if we haven't published randomized controlled trials proving it yet.

On the question of which certification criteria should be removed: I would strongly caution against removing transparency and auditability requirements on the assumption that outcome literature doesn't yet exist. That logic inverts what we know. Let the standards stay in place, let them generate real-world experience, and then measure what happens. Don't use the newness of the standards as justification to dismantle them before we have any chance to learn whether they work.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The commenter urges retention of AI model card disclosure requirements in health IT certification, arguing that transparency about algorithmic limitations and training data is essential for clinicians to safely evaluate whether systems are appropr...
Comment:
My mother's oncology team in Des Moines spent three weeks trying to obtain her pathology slides from an imaging center forty miles away. Those three weeks mattered. While we waited for copies on a USB drive, she waited to start treatment, and I watched my family navigate a healthcare system that couldn't seem to talk to itself even when lives were on the line. That experience is what brought me to this work, and it's why I'm deeply concerned about the current proposal to weaken AI transparency requirements in health IT certification.

The Alliance for Connected Care supports removing barriers to data sharing. We really do. Information blocking harms patients in concrete ways—we see it constantly in our member organizations. But I've also learned something harder in the past year: faster access to data without rigorous safeguards isn't automatically better. My mother's medical records were finally shared, yes, but I never learned what security measures were actually in place as those files moved between systems. I still don't know who had access to them, or whether the pathology lab and the oncology clinic had any real agreement about how to handle that information. Speed matters, but so does the ability to know what you're getting into when you share something as sensitive as someone's cancer diagnosis.

I'm writing to urge the Office of the National Coordinator to retain the current certification requirement that health IT developers disclose detailed AI model cards as part of the data sharing infrastructure (DSI) criterion. This isn't about slowing innovation or protecting legacy systems. It's about maintaining a minimal baseline of transparency in software that will make life-or-death decisions about which patients get which treatments.

Let me explain what I mean by stepping back to the problem itself. The proposal assumes that reducing compliance burden on developers will naturally increase competition, lower costs, and improve data sharing. That logic makes sense in many markets. But health IT isn't a normal market, and AI in clinical settings isn't a normal technology. When a developer builds an algorithm to identify which patients should be prioritized for a particular treatment pathway, or to flag which records are most relevant to a clinical question, those decisions embed assumptions about whose data was used to train the system, what outcomes it was optimized for, and what populations it was tested on. Those assumptions have real consequences for real patients.

My concern isn't theoretical. I've been reading through the clinical literature on algorithmic bias in healthcare, and the pattern is consistent: systems trained primarily on data from academic medical centers perform worse for rural patients. Systems optimized for one demographic group show higher error rates for others. A model that works reasonably well at Mayo Clinic might systematically misclassify conditions in a smaller hospital in Iowa. None of this means the algorithms are inherently bad—it means they have known limitations and known risks that depend entirely on context. A clinician needs to understand those limitations before relying on the system.

The current DSI certification requirement for AI model cards asks developers to disclose exactly that information: intended use, target population, known performance gaps, training data characteristics, validation processes, and residual risks. This isn't busywork. This is the minimum documentation needed for a healthcare provider to understand whether a system is actually appropriate for their patient population and their clinical workflows. Without it, we're asking clinicians to adopt software they can't evaluate, and we're asking patients to trust systems nobody is required to explain.

I recognize the concern about compliance burden. I do. Smaller developers have told us that detailed documentation requirements increase costs and slow time-to-market. That's real. But the solution isn't to eliminate the requirement—it's to get clearer about what's actually necessary and what's just procedural redundancy. The current model card requirement isn't excessive. It's asking for documentation that any competent developer should already have internally. The burden is mostly in formatting and disclosure, not in creating new knowledge.

Here's what troubles me about removing this requirement: it treats transparency as optional. It treats the documentation of what a system can and cannot do as a nice-to-have rather than a fundamental safety feature. And in healthcare, that's backwards. We don't let pharmaceutical companies sell drugs without disclosing what they were tested on and what the known risks are. We don't let medical device manufacturers sell diagnostic equipment without explaining the limits of accuracy. Why would we allow AI systems that make clinical recommendations to operate with less transparency than a blood pressure monitor?

The proposal also seems to assume that market forces and competitive pressure will somehow incentivize transparency on their own. I'm skeptical of that. In my experience, developers disclose what they're required to disclose, and they disclose it in the minimum format that satisfies the requirement. That's not a criticism of developers—they're responding to normal market incentives. But when information asymmetry favors the seller and the stakes are patient safety, requiring transparency isn

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: Removing transparency and disclosure requirements for healthcare AI systems won't streamline care but will instead slow adoption by eroding clinician trust, while the actual barriers to efficient care are outdated data transfer infrastructure and ...
Comment:
My wife switched cardiologists last year when our insurance changed, and getting her old test results transferred was a nightmare. Three weeks of phone calls, and in the end I had to drive a CD to the new doctor's office myself. I get why people want to cut through that kind of red tape.

But here's what worries me: I work in office management, and I know the difference between cutting waste and cutting corners. When you remove requirements for how AI systems in healthcare actually work—the transparency stuff like model cards and detailed disclosures—you're not streamlining anything. You're creating a different kind of bottleneck.

My wife's new cardiologist won't trust an algorithm if he can't understand how it makes recommendations. That means slower adoption, not faster. He'll keep doing things the old way because the risk of something going wrong without knowing why is too high. It's like asking someone to take a medication without knowing the side effects. Nobody does that.

The certification requirements aren't the problem slowing down care. Bad data transfer is the problem. Outdated fax machines are the problem. But removing transparency requirements for AI? That just means when something fails—and something will fail eventually—nobody will know why, and patients like my wife end up caught in the middle. 

Real innovation means building systems doctors can actually trust to use. That requires knowing how they work.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: While acknowledging the genuine burden of healthcare IT certification costs, the commenter argues that transparency requirements for clinical AI systems—particularly model card disclosures documenting intended use, limitations, and validation data...
Comment:
Five years ago, when we implemented our current EHR system, the certification process consumed eighteen months and nearly $2 million in compliance documentation. That was money we could have invested in clinical training, interoperability infrastructure, or staff development. I watched that cost, paid it, and learned something important: regulatory burden matters. What matters more, though, is what happens when you skip the transparency that costs you money upfront.

Last spring a health tech startup from Missoula pitched us on a patient-safety dashboard they'd built. The clinical team was excited. The data quality looked solid. But integrating it with our system would have required them to navigate certification pathways they couldn't afford. So we never got to use it. That's a real loss—not hypothetical, not theoretical. It's a startup that couldn't scale and a clinical tool we couldn't deploy. I understand why deregulation sounds appealing when you've paid those bills.

But here's what concerns me now as we move toward widespread AI integration in clinical decision support systems: we cannot afford to treat AI transparency the way we've treated other regulatory overhead. The certification costs we absorbed were real and frustrating. Model card disclosure requirements, though—documenting intended use, known limitations, validation processes, training data characteristics—these aren't bureaucratic padding. They're the documentation of what a system actually does and doesn't do in clinical settings.

I say this as someone skeptical of regulatory bloat. The real cost isn't disclosure; it's the alternative. It's a deployed system that fails silently in edge cases no one documented. It's liability cascading across institutions because the limitations weren't transparent. It's clinicians losing confidence in tools that could have helped them if we'd just been honest about what they were designed for and where they break.

We've learned the hard way that cheaper upfront doesn't mean cheaper overall. We need AI model card disclosures required now, before these systems proliferate across patient care. Not because regulation is inherently good, but because the cost of deployed failure is far higher than the cost of documented transparency. That's not a regulatory philosophy. That's math.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The Federation of State Medical Boards opposes weakening transparency and accountability requirements for clinical AI systems, arguing that strengthened model card disclosures and ongoing certification are essential safeguards to prevent algorithm...
Comment:
To the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology and the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy:

The Federation of State Medical Boards submits this comment on the proposed deregulatory framework for health IT certification and interoperability standards. We appreciate the Administration's focus on reducing unnecessary burden. However, we must express serious concern about proposals that would weaken transparency and accountability requirements for algorithmic systems in clinical settings. We urge a measured approach that preserves the safeguards patients depend on.

Last month, my daughter learned that her psychiatric records from 2015 were accessed by someone using credentials from a clinic she had never visited. A contractor's system had been breached. Nobody discovered it for three years. When she finally got answers, the clinic's response was that their security procedures "met standards at the time." She had to hire a lawyer to find out who accessed what. This is not a theoretical privacy concern. It is what happens when we prioritize streamlined access over the actual infrastructure that prevents misuse.

On the question of which certification criteria should be removed or revised: we believe model card disclosures should be strengthened, not eliminated or weakened. Model cards are not bureaucratic overhead. They are the minimum transparency standard needed to prevent silent system failures and algorithmic harm. When a clinical AI system makes decisions about patient care—medication recommendations, diagnostic support, treatment planning—someone needs to be able to audit what that system is actually doing. Without detailed model documentation, there is no way to detect whether an algorithm is being applied appropriately, whether it is perpetuating bias, or whether it has been compromised. This is not innovation theater. It is accountability.

The "black box" problem in healthcare AI directly enables the kind of unauthorized access and unaccountable data use my daughter experienced. If regulators and clinicians cannot see how algorithmic systems process patient information, they cannot audit for misuse. They cannot trace what went wrong after something breaks. Patients have no recourse. Removing detailed certification criteria would repeat the pattern of "standards at the time" excuses that leave patients defenseless.

We also urge caution regarding proposals to reduce conditions and maintenance of certification requirements. I understand the appeal of this framing. Compliance costs money. But in healthcare, the cost of weak standards is borne by patients, not by developers. We need stronger safeguards for clinical AI, not weaker ones. Model cards, transparency requirements, and ongoing certification are the floor. We should be raising that floor, not lowering it in the name of deregulation.

Respectfully submitted,

James Clark
Senior Policy Analyst
Federation of State Medical Boards
Maryland

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The commenter supports deregulation to enable smaller EHR vendors to compete, but opposes removing AI transparency requirements, arguing that eliminating disclosure standards for model performance and validation data creates blind spots for clinic...
Comment:
Thank you for requesting input on the deregulatory framework for health IT certification and maintenance requirements. I want to raise a concern that's been weighing on me for the better part of a year, watching how these changes may play out on the ground.

Last spring, our clinic network was approached by a smaller EHR vendor whose platform had some genuinely innovative features we needed—better clinical workflow integration, smarter alert logic that our staff actually found useful rather than disruptive. But they couldn't afford the full certification pathway under current requirements. The vendor estimated certification costs would run into the hundreds of thousands, plus the timeline would push us out another 18 months. We couldn't wait. So we stuck with our legacy system, which costs us roughly 18% of our operational budget annually and does half of what we wanted. That's the frustrating part: the better solution lost out not because it was inferior, but because compliance barriers priced out innovation.

Now I'm seeing the proposed relief, and honestly, it worries me. If we're removing AI model card disclosure requirements and trimming certification standards broadly, I understand the intent—let smaller developers move faster. But here's what I'm afraid will actually happen: the large vendors have compliance infrastructure already in place. They have legal teams, regulatory experts, the institutional knowledge to navigate whatever remains. When you reduce barriers, they scale down their compliance costs by a smaller percentage than a startup does. Meanwhile, the smaller innovators we need competing in this space still face real friction because they're starting from zero.

The piece that troubles me most is removing transparency requirements around AI model performance and validation data. Our clinicians are making patient care decisions based on algorithmic recommendations now. If we can't access basic performance information—what the system was trained on, where it fails, what biases were tested for—how are we supposed to responsibly deploy it? We're supposed to monitor for safety issues, but that's blind monitoring. A clinician using an algorithm without knowing its limitations isn't practicing carefully; they're practicing recklessly, and they don't even know it.

What would actually help: keep the disclosure requirements for AI-driven clinical functions, but simplify or streamline the certification process itself. Let smaller vendors get to market faster on the infrastructure side. Require transparency where patient safety depends on understanding how a tool works. That's not burden for its own sake; that's basic risk management.

I want genuine competition. I want that smaller vendor to succeed because their product is better, not because they got a regulatory waiver that larger competitors had already factored into their business model. Right now, I'm worried the opposite will happen.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The commenter supports reducing regulatory burden on health IT developers but warns against selective or uneven deregulation that would disadvantage smaller vendors who already invested in rigorous compliance; they advocate for uniform deregulatio...
Comment:
Thank you for the opportunity to comment on this proposed deregulation framework. I want to be direct: I'm supportive of reducing unnecessary burden on health IT developers, but I'm worried about how this gets implemented, and I think the approach matters as much as the outcome.

At iPill, we spent eighteen months and nearly $400,000 getting our medication-management platform through certification before we could contract with rural clinics. We built detailed AI model cards, documented our algorithm's limitations, trained clinicians on when the system should and shouldn't be trusted. It was expensive and it was slow, but we did it because we believed—still believe—that in healthcare, people have a right to know whether a recommendation came from a human expert or an algorithm, and what that algorithm's blind spots are.

Then I watched a larger competitor launch essentially the same feature set in six months with half the overhead. They had better lawyers. Better lobbyists. Different timing with regulators. I don't know exactly what their transparency requirements were, but the asymmetry was obvious.

Here's what concerns me about uneven deregulation: if you lower the bar for everyone uniformly, fine. We adapt, we rebuild, we compete on features and service. But if you lower it selectively—if larger vendors get exemptions or phase-in periods while smaller vendors who already invested in rigorous compliance get stuck with outdated costs—then you haven't reduced burden. You've just redistributed it downward. You've punished the vendors who tried to do this right the first time.

On the specific questions about which criteria to revise: I'm hesitant to say "remove AI transparency requirements" outright. That sounds good in theory until a clinician in a rural emergency department doesn't know that an AI recommendation is unreliable for their patient population, and something bad happens. What I'd rather see is this—if you're streamlining certification requirements, make the streamlining available to all vendors at the same time, with the same timeline, under the same conditions. No grandfather clauses. No competitive exemptions. Make it actually uniform, not uniform in name while vendors negotiate case-by-case relief.

The other thing: conditions and maintenance of certification are expensive, yes. But in high-stakes healthcare, they're not just bureaucracy. They're how you know a system is still working, still reliable, still being monitored. I'd rather see smarter maintenance—risk-based, outcome-focused—than wholesale removal. An algorithm that screens for sepsis should have stricter ongoing monitoring than one that helps schedule appointments.

I want innovation. I want faster time to market. I also want patients and clinicians to know what they're trusting their care to. Those things don't have to conflict, but they will if deregulation becomes a way for deep-pocketed competitors to outrun smaller vendors who invested in doing things carefully. That's not competition on merit. That's competition on leverage.

My wife was diagnosed with diabetes last year, and we went to three different doctors trying to get her sorted out. Every single time we walked into a new office, they said they couldn't access her previous test results. We'd have to explain everything all over again, answer the same questions, get the same tests repeated. It was frustrating and wasteful, and honestly it made me worry—what if someone missed something important because they didn't have the full picture?

That experience stuck with me, and now I'm thinking about the hospitals and clinics that are going to be using these new AI tools to help diagnose and treat patients. Here's what concerns me: how can a hospital administrator or a doctor know if an AI tool is actually safe for their patients if they don't get clear information about how it was built, what it's supposed to do, who it was tested on, and what could go wrong? It's the same problem we had with my wife's records, just in a different form.

When you strip away the transparency requirements, you're basically saying hospitals have to make big decisions about patient safety without the information they need. Procurement teams can't compare tools. Doctors can't understand the risks. It puts patients at risk just like fragmented medical records do.

I'm not against reducing red tape for developers. But transparency about how an AI model works—intended use, target population, training data, validation—that's not burden. That's basic information that healthcare systems need to protect patients. Please keep those requirements in place.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: Maintaining transparency and disclosure requirements for AI medical devices is essential for patient safety, as lack of visibility into how AI systems work—similar to the fragmented medical records that nearly caused harm to the commenter's mother...
Comment:
My mom had a stroke last year and spent three weeks in the hospital. What scared me most wasn't the stroke itself—it was watching five different doctors work with her medical records like they were pieces of a puzzle that didn't fit together. One doctor prescribed a medication she's allergic to because another doctor's notes never made it to his screen. Nobody could explain why the systems didn't talk to each other. I just kept thinking, what if that allergy note had been delayed one more day? What if we didn't catch it in time?

I understand the goal here is to make things easier and faster for hospitals and companies developing health software. I'm not against that. But I need to be honest about what worries me: if you start removing requirements for AI transparency in medical devices, you're basically asking doctors to use tools without knowing how they actually work or what they might get wrong. That's exactly the kind of corner-cutting that almost hurt my mom.

When hospitals can't see detailed information about how an AI system was trained or tested, they can't catch problems before those problems hurt someone. Doctors need to understand the technology they're relying on. Model cards, disclosure requirements—these aren't just bureaucratic red tape. They're the safety net.

I'm asking you not to let companies hide how their AI systems work just to save money or speed things up. Patients are counting on doctors to know what they're using. Please don't take that away.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: BeHuman Corp. argues that comprehensive AI model card disclosure requirements for health IT certification should be retained because they ensure vendor transparency about algorithm limitations and performance gaps, enabling healthcare systems to m...
Comment:
Thank you for the opportunity to comment on the proposed modifications to health IT certification requirements. We are writing on behalf of beHuman Corp., a health IT company based in Texas that develops patient scheduling and interoperability solutions for mid-sized healthcare systems.

I want to be direct about what prompted us to submit this comment. Last year we invested eighteen months and nearly $400,000 to achieve certification for our scheduling module under the current DSI certification criterion—specifically the requirement for detailed AI model card disclosures that document intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and validation processes. We did this because we believed that standard was necessary, and frankly, because we had no choice if we wanted our product to be trusted by the systems we were trying to integrate with. Now, as we hear that the agency is considering streamlined approval pathways that would allow competitors to bypass substantial portions of these requirements through alternative API standards, I'll be honest: my team and I are concerned we got caught holding an expensive bag while the rules change.

But that concern, while real and worth acknowledging, isn't actually the core of what we need to say here. What matters more is that the current DSI certification criterion requiring comprehensive AI model card disclosure is doing exactly what it's supposed to do—it's forcing vendors like us to be transparent about what our algorithms actually do, who they work for, and where they might fail. In healthcare, that transparency isn't a competitive burden. It's the foundation for patient safety and clinical trust.

Let me explain why this matters by walking through what happened in our certification process. Our scheduling algorithm uses machine learning to predict no-show rates based on patient history, appointment type, and demographic factors. On the surface, that sounds straightforward. But the devil is in the details. We had to document that our training data came primarily from three large health systems in the Southeast, which meant our model performed differently for rural populations and smaller practices. We discovered a 12% performance gap for certain age groups. We had to be explicit about that. We had to detail our validation methodology, which included holdout testing across different patient populations and clinic types. We had to identify the failure modes—the scenarios where the algorithm simply doesn't work well—and flag them clearly.

That process was expensive and time-consuming. Our compliance team spent months working with our data scientists to produce documentation that met the criterion. But here's what actually happened as a result: when we pitched this product to health systems, we weren't asking them to trust our marketing claims. We were handing them a detailed technical document that let their IT directors and clinical leaders understand exactly what the product would and wouldn't do in their specific setting. Several prospects actually used that model card to decide whether our solution was appropriate for their patient population. One large system in Texas chose not to implement our scheduling module because the documentation made clear our model had known limitations with their rural clinic population. That's the system working as intended.

I understand the argument for streamlining requirements. Faster market entry, lower costs for developers, more competition. These are not trivial concerns. But in healthcare AI, where algorithms influence decisions about patient care—even something as seemingly simple as appointment scheduling can affect access to care—transparency requirements aren't bureaucratic overhead. They're a mechanism for accountability. When a clinician or administrator is evaluating whether to deploy an AI system in their workflow, they need to know what it was built on, what it's been tested against, and what the known limitations are. Without that information, they're not making an informed decision. They're just hoping the vendor is trustworthy.

What worries me about the proposed changes is that they appear to create a two-tier system. Companies that use certain API standards would face lighter certification requirements, while vendors using other technical approaches would retain the full burden. That's not actually deregulation—that's regulatory arbitrage. It's a way of saying that the burden doesn't go down; it just shifts. And it creates a perverse incentive for vendors to choose their technical architecture based on which pathway requires less disclosure, rather than based on what actually works best for interoperability and patient safety.

We're also concerned about what happens when a less-scrutinized AI system gets deployed and fails in ways that weren't documented because the vendor wasn't required to do the analysis. Who bears that risk? The health system that deployed it bears the risk. The patients affected by incorrect clinical decisions bear the risk. The vendor that cut corners on validation to get to market faster? They're already profitable by then.

I don't think this is an argument for keeping every existing requirement exactly as written. There may be inefficiencies in the current process. There may be ways to streamline documentation without sacrificing substance. But the specific requirement for comprehensive AI model card disclosure is doing essential work. It's making vendors think carefully about whether our algorithms

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050

Comment:
Thank you for considering comments on the proposed deregulatory actions. iPill has spent the last eighteen months navigating the DSI certification pathway for our medication-adherence application, and I want to be direct about what concerns me most as you move forward with these changes.

The current requirement for detailed AI model card disclosures—intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, validation processes—feels burdensome when you're a small team trying to get to market. I understand that friction. We felt it acutely. But I've also watched clinicians at partner health systems ask exactly the right questions about our algorithms because those disclosures were there. A physician at Iowa Methodist last month caught a potential issue with our model's performance in patients over 65 on multiple medications because the validation data was transparent. She flagged it. We fixed it before deployment. That conversation wouldn't have happened without the certification requirements.

What worries me about loosening AI model transparency is that we're solving for the wrong problem. Yes, smaller vendors need relief—I've been there, watching our budget get eaten by compliance. But the answer isn't less transparency. It's streamlined transparency. You could require model cards without requiring the eighteen-month timeline. You could create a standardized template that takes weeks, not months, to complete. The burden isn't inherently the disclosure itself; it's the current process.

If we remove detailed AI disclosures entirely, we're handing an advantage to larger vendors who can absorb regulatory risk and liability that smaller companies cannot. I can tell you from experience: patient safety and clinical adoption both depend on trust, and trust requires knowing how an algorithm actually works—what it's trained on, where it might fail, who it was validated in. Clinicians won't adopt tools they don't understand. And when adoption stalls, the innovation argument falls apart.

I'm not opposed to reducing burden. I'm asking you to reduce process burden without reducing transparency. Model cards matter in healthcare AI in ways they might not in other sectors.

Linda Martinez
VP Regulatory Affairs
iPill Inc.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: TMF Health Quality Institute opposes deregulatory actions that would eliminate AI model card disclosure requirements, arguing that the absence of published outcome studies does not prove these transparency mechanisms lack value, and that removing ...
Comment:
RE: Request for Information – Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions

TMF Health Quality Institute appreciates the opportunity to respond to this RFI, particularly given our work supporting quality measurement and health data integrity across provider networks. We write with concern about a specific framing that appears in the deregulatory discussion: the notion that absence of published outcome studies on transparency mechanisms—particularly AI model card disclosures—constitutes evidence that these mechanisms lack real-world value. We believe this conflates two very different conclusions and risks dismantling accountability tools at the precise moment healthcare AI adoption is accelerating.

Last month, a patient contacted our office describing a six-week struggle to retrieve her surgical history from a hospital system across town. Her cardiologist's records were incomplete when she finally obtained them. She had already moved to a different provider and ended up repeating diagnostic tests. This experience crystallized something we hear often: patients understand intuitively that they need transparency and control over their information. What they're asking for—and what AI model card requirements are meant to provide—is not just access but understanding. When a clinical algorithm influences a treatment recommendation, patients and their doctors deserve to know how that algorithm was trained, what its performance characteristics are, and what assumptions it carries.

The RFI suggests that detailed disclosures of AI model characteristics impose compliance burden without demonstrated clinical benefit. But here's what concerns us: these transparency requirements are genuinely new. We do not yet have longitudinal outcome data because the field itself is still ramping up. The absence of published studies does not prove ineffectiveness; it reflects that we are living through the early adoption phase. To remove these disclosure requirements now—before we have real-world evidence of their impact—would be like dismantling safety railings while the building is still under construction because we don't yet have a published study proving that railings save lives.

We urge the agencies to preserve AI model card transparency as a certification requirement, particularly for algorithms that inform clinical decisions. If compliance costs are the concern, we support streamlined submission formats or tiered disclosure requirements based on algorithm risk. But removing the requirement itself creates a gap in accountability at exactly the wrong moment in this technology's maturity curve.

We are happy to discuss further.

Respectfully,

Daniel Miller
Executive Director
TMF Health Quality Institute
Seattle, WA

My daughter switched pediatricians last spring when our insurance changed, and getting her vaccination records transferred took three weeks. Three weeks. Her new doctor couldn't see anything from her previous visit, and I was terrified we'd miss something important—a reaction, a contraindication, anything. Removing transparency requirements for AI model cards means doctors are flying blind. They can't check whether a system has been tested for the populations they serve. They can't catch errors before those errors become diagnoses. That's not reducing burden—that's hiding risk. Data sharing only works if both sides trust the system, and trust requires knowing how it works. Please don't trade patient safety for developer convenience.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: Loosening EHR certification and transparency requirements before establishing a research infrastructure to measure their effects creates a self-fulfilling prophecy that prevents the generation of evidence needed to evaluate whether cost savings co...
Organization Name: NORC at the University of Chicago
Comment:
Last spring, my team at NORC was deep into a longitudinal study tracking EHR adoption across rural Michigan clinics—six sites, three years of data collection planned. We had just entered year two when three of our partner clinics switched to uncertified systems because the licensing costs were unsustainable. I spent weeks on the phone trying to understand what had changed. Turns out certification standards had been quietly loosened. Now I'm staring at a dataset with gaping holes right when we're supposed to be publishing results about whether those cost savings actually harm patient safety.

This experience crystallizes the core problem with the proposed deregulation. The agency asserts that transparency requirements—particularly AI model card disclosures—lack empirical support, but that conflates two very different things: the absence of published outcome studies with the absence of real-world benefit. AI governance literature in healthcare is barely five years old. Of course we don't have robust longitudinal evidence on model card transparency yet. That lag between policy implementation and measurable outcomes is not a flaw in the evidence base; it's inherent to how medical research actually works.

What concerns me is the self-fulfilling prophecy this creates. If you remove detailed transparency requirements before we have the ability to study their effects, you eliminate the research infrastructure needed to generate the very comparative effectiveness data the agency says doesn't exist. Researchers can't harmonize datasets across systems with different disclosure standards. We can't track whether cheaper uncertified systems correlate with worse outcomes because we don't have the metadata to conduct that analysis.

I'm not arguing against innovation or cost reduction. I'm arguing that removing certification standards before adequate evidence exists to evaluate them prevents the evidence generation we actually need. My Michigan clinics taught me that cost savings and patient safety aren't automatically opposed—but we can't know which is which if interoperability requirements are stripped away before we can measure the difference.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: XSELL Technologies opposes lowering AI transparency requirements in health IT certification, arguing that removing proactive disclosure mandates prioritizes innovation over patient safety and creates unfair competitive advantage for new entrants w...
Comment:
Thank you for the opportunity to comment on the proposed revisions to health IT certification criteria. I want to be direct about what concerns me here, because this isn't just about regulatory efficiency—it's about patient safety and fairness to vendors who've already done the work.

XSELL Technologies spent eighteen months and nearly two million dollars obtaining full certification under the current framework for our patient-management software. We built rigorous AI transparency protocols into our system because we believed—and still believe—that in healthcare, you don't wait for patient harm to document itself before requiring disclosure. Model cards, training data transparency, bias assessments: these aren't bureaucratic theater. They're the difference between a clinician understanding what their tool might miss and discovering it through a preventable medical error. The moment you remove proactive AI disclosure requirements, you're betting that innovation matters more than preventing harm before it reaches the clinic. I can't support that bet when patients are the stakes.

But there's another problem I can't ignore. You're proposing to lower barriers that competitors haven't yet had to clear. That's not deregulation in good faith—that's creating a two-tiered market where the vendors who invested heavily in doing this right are now locked into expensive compliance while new entrants skip the same work and launch in six weeks. We absorbed that cost because we thought it was the standard. Now you're telling us there was never a binding standard at all. That's not innovation. That's competitive arbitrage, and it punishes the vendors who moved first.

If you're going to revise certification criteria, do it consistently. Either AI transparency requirements are essential—in which case they apply to everyone—or they're not. Don't create a regulatory class system where my compliance obligation disappears for the startup launching next month. That's not fair competition; it's regulatory whiplash that rewards speed over integrity.

On your specific questions: Rather than removing transparency criteria entirely, consider streamlining them. Require model cards for AI-driven clinical decisions, but consolidate duplicate documentation requirements across different certification pathways. Keep interoperability standards firm—they're the connective tissue of health systems—but let vendors choose implementation methods rather than dictating technical specifications. And please, maintain ongoing transparency obligations for maintenance of certification. That's where problems surface in real clinical use.

The question isn't whether health IT innovation matters. Of course it does. The question is whether we're willing to require that innovation to be transparent about what it can and cannot do. I believe we are. I also believe vendors who've already invested in that transparency shouldn't be penalized while new competitors avoid the same burden.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: Safety-net clinics need robust AI transparency standards like model cards to independently evaluate algorithms they lack resources to audit, while the actual regulatory burden comes from vendor lock-in and interoperability refusal that fragments p...
Organization Name: South Carolina Primary Health Care Association
Comment:
On behalf of the South Carolina Primary Health Care Association, I want to address the proposed regulatory changes with a perspective grounded in what actually happens in safety-net clinics when we try to care for vulnerable patients.

Last year, I coordinated care transitions for our network of community health centers. We saw a patient with poorly controlled diabetes referred to an endocrinologist whose EHR couldn't talk to ours. Not because the technology didn't exist, but because the vendor contracts made interoperability expensive and unprofitable. She repeated lab work. Missed medication adjustments. This happens constantly in our clinics, and it's not a technology problem—it's a business incentive problem. But I'm concerned that the current RFI approach conflates two very different kinds of burden: vendor lock-in practices that actually block care, and transparency standards that help clinicians understand what they're using.

The question about which certification criteria should be revised to reduce burden misses something important. Model cards for AI algorithms aren't a new compliance layer—they're documentation standards that help our clinicians understand and trust tools they're skeptical about. In safety-net settings, we already face adoption friction because vendors have burned us before. Removing detailed AI disclosure requirements would leave our smaller clinics and advocacy organizations unable to independently audit algorithms that could harm the populations we serve. We don't have in-house data scientists. We have limited IT staff. When a vendor says "trust this algorithm," we need some way to actually evaluate that claim.

The real burden I'm watching isn't transparency documentation. It's redundant certifications, vendor refusal to interoperate, and EHR systems designed to lock in clients rather than serve patients. Those are what drain resources from our clinical staff. Model cards actually reduce adoption barriers by demystifying algorithms—they build the trust we need to implement tools thoughtfully.

I'd suggest the deregulatory focus target the practices that genuinely fragment care and force workarounds: vendor lock-in, interoperability refusal, certification redundancy. Keep the transparency standards that help under-resourced clinics make informed decisions about high-risk tools. That's not burden—that's equity.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: A hospital system leader argues that weakening AI model card disclosure requirements in EHR certification would harm patient safety, disadvantage smaller vendors who have already invested in compliance, and entrench market dominance by larger vend...
Comment:
Sandra Green
RubyWell, Inc.
Birmingham, Alabama

RE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions

Thank you for the opportunity to comment on the proposed revisions to EHR certification and conditions of maintenance. RubyWell operates a 420-bed hospital system in central Alabama, and we've spent the last eighteen months navigating the economics of health IT procurement in ways that have made the stakes of this rulemaking very clear to us.

Last year, we evaluated an EHR system from a smaller vendor whose product was technically superior to what we ultimately purchased from Epic. Their architecture was cleaner. Their interoperability features worked better out of the box. But they couldn't afford the certification pathway to get on our procurement list. They were burning through capital just trying to meet ONC standards while competing against vendors with ten times their revenue. So we did what every other large health system does: we defaulted to Epic. The superior product didn't matter because the regulatory cost of entry had already decided the outcome.

That vendor is finally raising the capital to pursue certification. They've committed to the investment. And now I'm watching the goalposts shift mid-race, and I need to be direct about what worries me: removing or significantly weakening AI model card disclosure requirements will compound the exact problem we just experienced.

Here's what I mean. If we eliminate requirements for vendors to disclose intended use, target populations, known performance risks, and validation methodologies for algorithmic decision support, we accomplish three things simultaneously. First, we strip clinicians of the performance and risk data we need to safely deploy these tools at the bedside. Second, we undercut smaller vendors who've already invested years and capital meeting current transparency standards. And third, we entrench the players who can afford to absorb regulatory whiplash because they've already locked in market share.

Patient safety is not abstract here. We use clinical algorithms to flag sepsis risk, predict readmission, and guide medication dosing. If I don't know how a model was trained, what population it was validated on, or where it fails, I cannot monitor for algorithmic harm. I cannot counsel my clinicians on when to override it. I cannot explain to a patient why a decision was made. Removing model cards doesn't reduce burden—it transfers risk from vendors onto hospitals and patients.

The question about certification criteria revision is where this matters most. We support streamlining unnecessary procedural overhead. But transparency requirements for algorithmic systems are not procedural overhead. They're the foundation of safe implementation. They're also what allows a smaller vendor with superior technology to compete on merit rather than budget.

What we need is stability. Vendors need to know that if they invest in compliance, the goalposts won't move. Clinicians need to know that the tools we're deploying have been scrutinized. Patients deserve that much.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050

Comment:
Polimorphic, Inc. submits these comments regarding the proposed revisions to health IT certification and interoperability requirements.

Our company develops EHR modules and related clinical decision support tools. We understand the intent behind streamlining certification—reducing unnecessary friction around compliance does matter—but I want to flag a specific concern that I don't think gets adequate attention in these discussions. Two years ago we spent eighteen months and roughly $1.9 million getting our EHR module certified. We did this partly because we believed it was the right thing and partly because we thought market leadership required it. A competitor launched a functionally similar product in half that time last month with a fraction of those compliance costs. The business math works: they're undercutting us on price and gaining early adoption. I'm not going to pretend this doesn't frustrate me. But what actually keeps me up is not the competitive disadvantage—it's what happens when clinicians can't see inside the AI models we're all deploying.

The proposed rule removes model card disclosure requirements for AI-driven diagnostic and treatment recommendations. I need to be direct: that's a mistake, and I say this as someone who would probably benefit competitively from looser rules.

Model cards aren't compliance overhead. They're the mechanism that allows a clinician to understand how an algorithm performs across different patient populations, where it fails, and whether those failure modes matter in their specific clinical context. When we built our sepsis prediction module, we discovered through real-world testing that performance degraded substantially in patients under 50. That's not something a clinician would discover through normal use—they'd discover it after harm occurred. The model card made that visible. It also made adoption easier because physicians trusted what they were deploying.

If we remove that requirement, what incentivizes vendors to run that testing? Faster time to market. Lower costs. What happens to clinicians? They inherit the risk. They're the ones explaining to families why an algorithm missed something it should have caught. They're the ones liable when a system fails silently.

I recognize the certification process is expensive and that smaller companies face real barriers. But the answer isn't to eliminate transparency—it's to streamline how transparency gets documented and verified. Require model cards, yes. But establish a standardized format so vendors aren't spending resources on redundant documentation. Let third-party labs certify model testing rather than forcing every vendor to replicate ONC's work.

We're going to deploy more algorithms in clinical settings, not fewer. The question is whether clinicians have the information they need to use them responsibly. I've invested in that standard because I believe patients are safer when clinicians understand what they're working with. That shouldn't be a competitive disadvantage.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The commenter opposes removing AI model card disclosure requirements from ONC certification criteria, arguing that transparency mandates for AI systems should precede deployment rather than follow documented failures, and that eliminating these re...
Comment:
Re: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity

Ad Hoc LLC submits this comment regarding proposed revisions to ONC certification criteria, with particular focus on the treatment of artificial intelligence transparency requirements in Developer Systems Information (DSI) certification.

Over the past eighteen months, I've worked with several health IT organizations navigating certification pathways. One startup in Tampa—led by a former resident of mine—developed a clinically validated EHR module that performed well in real-world testing. They couldn't afford the certification costs and pathway complexity. Within months, a significantly larger vendor released a functionally similar product that moved through the same regulatory environment with substantially fewer apparent obstacles. I don't know exactly how that happened, but the asymmetry troubles me. It suggests the burden of proof falls unevenly, depending on who you are and what resources you have.

That experience shapes how I read the proposal to reduce certification criteria and streamline maintenance-of-certification requirements. I understand the impulse. Compliance is genuinely expensive. But there's a specific area where I think the deregulatory logic breaks down: AI model card disclosure requirements in DSI certification.

The reasoning I keep hearing is that we should remove upfront transparency mandates and require disclosure only after documented clinical failures occur. That's backwards. In healthcare, we don't wait for preventable harms to force action—we require safety evidence before deployment. Model cards documenting intended use, target populations, training data characteristics, known performance limitations, and validation methodology aren't bureaucratic niceties. They're the clinical equivalent of precondition validation. When an AI algorithm informs triage decisions or flags deterioration risk, clinicians need to know what it was designed to do, who it was validated on, and what failure modes have been identified.

Without these criteria, the certification pathway becomes permissive for vendors with resources to navigate ambiguity, while smaller organizations face informal pressure to over-disclose. That recreates the inequity I witnessed in Tampa. More importantly, it means we're deliberately choosing to learn what AI systems can't do by harming patients first, rather than demanding that developers demonstrate what they can do responsibly from the outset.

I would urge retention and modest strengthening of AI model card criteria in DSI certification, with clear specifications for what constitutes adequate documentation of clinical validation. The burden reduction should come from streamlining the submission process and timeline, not from eliminating transparency requirements that directly protect patient safety.

Thank you for considering this perspective.

Thomas Lewis
Chief Medical Officer
Ad Hoc LLC
Florida

Conditions of Maintenance requirements could be consolidated where they overlap, but maintaining granular interoperability standards serves everyone—especially smaller developers who need clear targets to hit.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: While supporting regulatory streamlining for health IT, the commenter urges agencies to retain detailed AI transparency requirements (model cards) for clinical decision-support systems, arguing that clinicians need clear documentation of a tool's ...
Comment:
RE: Request for Information Related to Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions

Thank you for the opportunity to comment on the proposed streamlining of certification requirements. The Digital Medicine Society appreciates the Administration's commitment to reducing unnecessary regulatory friction in health IT development.

My perspective on this issue is shaped partly by professional experience and partly by something more immediate. My mother was diagnosed with early-stage Alzheimer's last year. Her neurologist practices in Minneapolis; her primary care physician is in Sioux Falls, where she lives. Over the past twelve months, we've encountered exactly the kind of fragmentation these rules are meant to address. Getting her records transferred between EHR systems has taken months. Her cognitive decline doesn't pause for administrative delays, and neither does the window for early interventions that might slow her disease progression. That's what makes this conversation real for me.

I want to be clear: I understand the innovation argument for streamlining certification. Developers face compliance costs, and those costs can slow deployment of tools that could help people. But I'd urge the agencies not to remove the detailed AI transparency requirements—the model cards that specify intended use, target population, known limitations, and training data characteristics. Here's why this matters concretely.

When my mother's care teams need to evaluate an AI-assisted diagnostic tool or a clinical decision support system, they need to know what that system was designed to do. An algorithm trained primarily on younger patients with different symptom presentations may fail silently on an 79-year-old with atypical disease progression. A tool validated on data from academic medical centers may not perform the same way in a smaller rural clinic. Her neurologist in Minneapolis and her primary care doctor in Sioux Falls can't coordinate her care safely if they can't understand how the systems they're relying on actually work.

Transparency and interoperability aren't competing priorities. They're complementary. Removing certification criteria for detailed AI disclosures creates information asymmetries that leave clinicians flying blind. The very interoperability standards we're fighting for—seamless data exchange across fragmented systems—only work if clinicians can trust what they're seeing and understand how AI is processing that data.

I'd suggest a middle path: keep the model card requirements for AI systems used in clinical decision-making, particularly those that inform diagnosis or treatment selection for vulnerable populations. Reduce burden where possible, but preserve the documentation standards that help providers detect algorithmic failures before they harm patients. Developers with well-documented, transparent models actually have a competitive advantage in healthcare settings where clinicians and compliance officers are scrutinizing tool selection.

This isn't about blocking innovation. It's about making sure that when my mother's care team has to decide whether to trust a recommendation from a clinical support system, they have the information they need to make that decision safely.

Anthony Allen
Senior Policy Analyst
Digital Medicine Society
Sioux Falls, South Dakota

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050

Comment:
Thank you for the opportunity to comment on this proposed rule. I want to be direct: I'm concerned that removing model card disclosure requirements will repeat the exact vendor lock-in problem my clinic already experienced, and this time at scale across health systems that can't afford to absorb the cost.

Two years ago we switched to a smaller vendor's scheduling platform because we couldn't justify the cost of the big players anymore. Within six months we hit a wall—their system didn't talk to our lab partners' software. There's no real standard between them. We ended up paying for custom patches, workarounds, and ultimately accepting data gaps that still frustrate our staff. If we'd known upfront what was actually under the hood of that system, what decisions shaped it, we might have made a different choice. What worries me now is that loosening certification criteria for AI tools will make this worse, not better.

Model card requirements aren't a burden on smaller vendors—they're actually the only thing protecting them. Right now, a small developer can compete on price and features because certification standards force everyone to document their work. Remove those transparency requirements and you accelerate a race to the bottom where only large vendors can afford the liability of poorly documented systems. A practice like mine will be forced to choose between expensive certified systems from the major players or unvetted AI tools with hidden design flaws. That's not competition. That's lock-in wearing a different mask.

What concerns me about revising conditions of certification is that you're essentially asking smaller clinics to trust the market to police itself. The market didn't protect us with that scheduling platform. We have no way to know what's actually in these systems unless someone makes developers tell us. Transparency standards like model cards level the playing field. They let smaller vendors prove they've done the work right. They let practices like mine make informed decisions instead of gambling on compatibility.

I understand the impulse to reduce burden on developers, but the burden shouldn't shift to us. We're already managing patches between incompatible programs while trying to deliver patient care. Before you remove these safeguards, ask yourselves: who benefits? It's not smaller practices. It's the vendors who can afford to cut corners and absorb the fallout.
