Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: A hospital CFO argues that AI model transparency requirements should be strengthened rather than weakened in certification rules, warning that removing disclosure standards without replacement will disadvantage smaller vendors and hospitals while ...
Comment:
As a hospital CFO who's spent the last five years untangling the fallout from certification requirements that made our 2019 EHR implementation cost $2.3 million more than expected—with those costs passed straight to us—I want to be direct about what concerns me with this rule.

We need AI model transparency more, not less, if we're going to make sound purchasing decisions. When vendors pitch us algorithmic tools for clinical workflows, we have almost no standardized way to understand what we're actually buying. What population was this model trained on? What are the known failure modes? How does it perform with our specific patient demographics? Right now, hospitals either trust marketing materials or we build our own validation studies, which is expensive and duplicative. A simple model card requirement—intended use, target population, training data characteristics, known risks, validation methodology—would let us evaluate fit for our actual workflows without reinventing the wheel every time.

The bigger worry I have is what happens when you remove these guardrails without replacing them. My experience with our last contract negotiation makes this concrete: a competitor's system with fewer certified modules undercut us by 40 percent because they hadn't invested in the same validation burden, but that cheaper option created data integration nightmares we're still managing four years later. Lower compliance costs sound great in theory, but not if it just means smaller vendors cut corners and larger ones absorb costs while lobbying to exempt themselves from disclosure requirements they can afford.

What actually levels the playing field isn't removing transparency requirements. It's keeping them. A smaller innovator with a genuinely better product can compete on merit if hospitals can see actual evidence of performance and safety. But if we strip away the disclosure requirements while keeping the technical standards, you've handed the advantage to whoever has the biggest lobbying budget and compliance department. We can absorb integration risk. Smaller systems can't.

I'd ask that any revisions to certification requirements preserve or strengthen the disclosure and transparency elements—not the certification timelines, which are genuinely burdensome, but the evidence standards that let us make informed decisions. That's the part that actually serves both patient safety and fair competition.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The commenter opposes reducing AI model transparency requirements in clinical decision-support tools, arguing that disclosure of how AI models work, their training data, and limitations is essential safety infrastructure for clinicians—not a burde...
Comment:
Last spring, my mother was diagnosed with atrial fibrillation. Her cardiologist in Salt Lake City wanted to consult with a specialist in Houston, and what should have been straightforward turned into three weeks of frustration. Faxes got lost. Digital systems wouldn't communicate with each other. She ended up repeating tests because no one could access her previous imaging. Watching her go through that—the anxiety, the wasted time, the redundant procedures—made me understand something about healthcare that I think gets lost in a lot of policy discussions: fragmentation hurts patients and clinicians alike.

Working in campaign strategy, I've learned that people often assume efficiency and safety are at odds. They're not, but only if you design for both. When I look at the current proposal to reduce certification requirements around AI model transparency in clinical decision-support tools, I worry we're making exactly that mistake.

The problem isn't that we need less interoperability. We need more—faster access to patient data where it matters. But removing the requirement for developers to disclose how their AI models work, what data trained them, and where they might fail for certain patient populations—that's not efficiency. That's risk.

A cardiologist using an AI tool to interpret rhythm strips needs to know if that tool was trained primarily on data from younger, white patients. An oncologist using predictive algorithms needs to understand their limitations before trusting them with treatment decisions. These aren't burdensome transparency requirements; they're basic infrastructure for informed clinical decision-making. Without them, clinicians are flying blind.

I understand that compliance costs money and that developers face real pressures. But the answer can't be to strip away the safeguards that help catch algorithmic errors before they hurt someone. That's especially true for vulnerable populations—patients with rare conditions, people from underrepresented groups in training datasets, elderly patients with complex medication histories. AI bias isn't theoretical. It happens, and model cards are how we detect it.

The RFI asks which certification criteria should be revised while maintaining interoperability. Here's what I'd suggest: keep the transparency requirements intact. Instead, look at streamlining the administrative burden of compliance—standardized templates for model documentation, faster review cycles, clearer guidance on what "adequate disclosure" actually means. That reduces developer burden without sacrificing the safety mechanisms clinicians depend on.

Efficiency and transparency aren't competing. They're connected. You can't have trustworthy interoperability without knowing how the systems processing that data actually work. Patients deserve both.

Abstract: The Coalition for Health AI supports streamlining compliance burdens but urges preservation of model card disclosure requirements, arguing that transparency about AI system design and limitations is essential for enabling adoption in rural and und...
Comment:
Re: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability—Request for Information on Certification and Maintenance of Certification Requirements

The Coalition for Health AI appreciates the opportunity to comment on this important initiative. We represent health systems, clinics, and patient advocacy organizations working to expand access to beneficial AI tools in clinical settings, particularly in underserved regions. We support streamlining unnecessary compliance burdens. However, we urge careful consideration of which requirements genuinely duplicate effort versus those that address real adoption barriers for frontline providers.

Last year, a rural diabetes clinic in western Kansas wanted to help a patient share glucose data with an endocrinologist three hours away. The patient faced a six-hour drive for what could have been a remote visit. What stopped them wasn't one regulation—it was the cumulative opacity around how the systems worked, who owned the liability if something went wrong, and whether the clinic could actually trust the AI tools being proposed for integration. We spent weeks just figuring out what rules applied.

This experience shapes how we think about deregulation. Model cards—the documentation that explains how an AI system was designed, what it was trained on, and what its known limitations are—aren't just compliance check boxes. For clinicians without AI expertise, they're the difference between feeling confident enough to deploy a tool and leaving it on the shelf. Small clinics can't hire data scientists to reverse-engineer a black box algorithm. When a rural provider is deciding whether an AI diagnostic aid is appropriate for their patient population, they need to know: Was this tested on populations like mine? What does the vendor actually claim it can do? What happens when it fails?

Removing detailed model card disclosure requirements would eliminate one of the few standards helping under-resourced clinics make informed adoption decisions. We're not asking for more red tape. We're asking that as you identify burdensome requirements to streamline, you preserve the transparency mechanisms that actually move rural and small-practice adoption forward.

We believe the real question is not whether to require disclosure, but whether current disclosure formats work for the clinicians who need them most. If the burden is in the format rather than the substance, revise the format. Don't abandon transparency itself.

My name is Lisa Adams, and I'm the practice manager at iPill inc in Missouri. We develop decision support integration products for mid-sized clinic networks. I need to flag something that's been keeping me up at night since we lost a contract renewal last month.

The clinic administrator asked why she should pay more for the same thing. I couldn't give her a good answer without walking her through technical validation details she didn't care about. That's a problem. We lost the contract.

What worries me more is that this will accelerate. If you remove model card disclosure requirements and loosen validation criteria before AI adoption becomes standard across clinic networks, you'll create a race to the bottom that responsible vendors can't win. My competitor is faster and cheaper because they're shipping unvalidated systems. Their clinic clients have no way to audit what those systems actually do. The clinic doesn't even know what questions to ask.

I understand the impulse to reduce certification burden. But removing transparency requirements before widespread AI adoption is the wrong time to do it. Once bad practices become industry standard, they're impossible to unwind. Clinics will be locked into systems they can't inspect or trust. And vendors who maintain rigorous validation will get priced out. That's not innovation. That's a compliance collapse.

On your specific questions about which criteria to revise: don't touch AI transparency and model card requirements. Those aren't bureaucratic overhead. They're the only mechanism clinics have to distinguish between validated and unvalidated systems. Everything else can be streamlined. But pull transparency standards now, and in three years you'll have a market full of black-box AI tools running in primary care with no audit trail and no way for clinics to know if they're actually working.

The irony is that strong transparency standards protect responsible vendors too. They create a competitive moat for companies that validate properly. Without them, the only competitive advantage is cost cutting. And there's always a competitor willing to cut further.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: SAFI Clinical Informatics Group argues against removing AI transparency requirements from health IT certification standards, warning that eliminating disclosure mandates for AI decision-support tools before documented failures occur will create a ...
Comment:
SAFI Clinical Informatics Group LLC submits this comment on the proposed modifications to health IT certification and information blocking requirements.

Our organization has spent the last eighteen months and nearly $2 million ensuring our latest EHR module meets current certification standards. That investment was substantial, but we made it because we believed—and still believe—that rigorous compliance protects patients. What troubles me about the current deregulatory proposal is not the impulse to reduce unnecessary burden. It's that removing transparency requirements around artificial intelligence in decision support products, before we've even documented widespread failures in clinical practice, risks creating exactly the two-tier market we're trying to avoid.

When we were in deep certification work, I watched a competitor launch a similar product in six months with a fraction of our overhead. The speed difference wasn't because they were smarter or more innovative. It was because they skipped the compliance work we considered non-negotiable. We absorbed that cost because we thought the rules protected everyone equally. But if transparency requirements for AI model disclosures are removed now, well-capitalized vendors like us can still afford rigorous internal governance. Smaller competitors facing margin pressure will cut corners on AI testing and documentation. That's not innovation. That's a race to the bottom where only firms that can afford expensive mistakes stay in the game.

The question the RFI raises about which certification criteria should be removed—I'd say: not the ones that require transparency about automated clinical decision-making. Those aren't arbitrary compliance taxes. They're guardrails that prevent a fragmented market where some vendors operate under strict AI governance and others operate under none.

Waiting for documented patient harm before requiring model card disclosures is ethically indefensible and economically irrational. Remediation costs after failures occur will dwarf the cost of transparency requirements now. We need to hold this line not because we want to burden innovators, but because we want a market where innovation doesn't require choosing between speed and responsibility.

Comment:
RE: Request for Information – Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity

My mother needed her medication records from her old doctor's office last year. It took three months. Three months of hold times, transferred calls, promises that "it's in the system somewhere." When the records finally arrived, the dosages were wrong. Her new doctor had to start over from scratch because nobody could tell us how the old system actually worked or why the information was corrupted in the first place.

That's what I keep thinking about when I read that we're removing transparency requirements from health IT certification. The language talks about reducing "burden" and "unleashing innovation," but what that actually means is less accountability for how these systems work—especially the AI tools that are now making real decisions about patient care.

I work with vulnerable patients across Nebraska. They don't have time to sit on hold for months. They can't afford to start medication regimens from scratch because a system was opaque. And they absolutely cannot be the ones discovering that an AI diagnostic tool was trained on data that doesn't represent their population, or that it systematically misses certain conditions.

Here's what concerns me about removing AI model card requirements: clinicians need to know what data trained an algorithm, what populations it was tested on, and what its failure modes are. That's not regulatory burden. That's basic due diligence. When a provider can't see how an AI system works, they can't catch errors before they harm someone. Patients lose the ability to ask informed questions or advocate for themselves—which is exactly what happened to my mother. She couldn't push back on wrong information because she didn't know the system was broken.

I understand developers want faster approval timelines. But "innovation" that hides how it works from the doctors using it isn't progress. It's a recipe for algorithmic bias and medical errors hitting the patients least equipped to recover from them.

If the goal is reducing unnecessary burden, focus on streamlining processes that don't matter—not transparency mechanisms that directly affect safety. Keep the model card requirements. Keep the standards that let patients access and move their own health data. Those aren't barriers to innovation. They're guardrails for accountability.

Respectfully,

Linda Jones
Community Organizer
National Council for Prescription Drug Programs
Lincoln, Nebraska

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050

Comment:
The Society of Thoracic Surgeons appreciates the opportunity to comment on this RFI. We represent over 7,000 cardiothoracic surgeons and surgical teams across the country, and our members operate at the intersection of clinical innovation and patient safety—a position that has given us hard-earned insight into what happens when regulatory clarity fails.

Last year, one of our member institutions experienced a breach that compromised not just patient medical records but the surgical scheduling infrastructure our team relies on daily. What followed was instructive and deeply troubling. When we tried to hold the vendor accountable—to understand what their system had failed to do and why—we found ourselves navigating overlapping regulations with conflicting requirements. The vendor's response was to claim that tightening security would reduce interoperability. The irony was painful: we were told that the only path forward was to accept less transparency about how their systems work, not more.

That experience shapes how we read the proposal to remove model card disclosure requirements under the DSI certification criterion. On the surface, I understand the deregulatory impulse. Nobody in my position wants unnecessary compliance burden. But I want to be direct: removing transparency requirements for AI and algorithmic systems used in high-stakes clinical decisions is not burden reduction. It's accountability abdication, and it will cost patients and advocacy organizations far more than it saves vendors.

Here's what concerns me most. When an algorithmic system makes a poor recommendation—when it misses a contraindication, when it systematically underperforms for a particular patient population—clinicians and patients have no documented baseline for understanding why. Was this a limitation the vendor knew about but didn't disclose? A training data bias? A validation gap? Without model cards and transparent disclosure of system limitations, you've shifted the entire burden of discovery onto the people harmed by the failure. That's not innovation. That's privatizing benefit and socializing cost.

The tension I face is real. I want interoperability. I want clinicians to access patient data across systems. I want competition and innovation in health IT. But I've learned that you don't achieve patient safety by removing the mechanisms that force vendors to be honest about what their systems can and cannot do. Model card disclosures under DSI certification aren't obstacles to innovation—they're the baseline for informed consent.

Regarding the specific RFI question about which certification criteria should be removed or revised: I would urge extreme caution with any revision that weakens transparency standards around algorithmic systems. The DSI model card requirement is not one of those criteria. If anything, it should expand as AI use in clinical decision support grows. The burden reduction should come from streamlining procedural compliance and redundancy elsewhere—not from erasing the documentation that keeps vendors accountable when systems fail.

What I've learned is that deregulation that weakens disclosure will disproportionately harm vulnerable populations who lack the technical literacy or institutional power to demand accountability after a breach. Advocacy organizations will inherit the cost of litigation and remediation that vendors should have borne through compliance.

I'm not opposed to thoughtful deregulation. But this particular change would be a mistake.

I spent weeks acting as an unpaid intermediary, manually reconciling information between hospitals, and I kept thinking—we've had the technical capability to solve this for at least a decade.

What I'm seeing now concerns me deeply. There's talk of removing or relaxing the certification criteria that require transparency around how these systems work, particularly the model card disclosures. I understand the argument that compliance is burdensome, but I've also seen firsthand what happens when vendors prioritize convenience over interoperability. The fragmentation isn't accidental—it's profitable. Lowering the bar for disclosure doesn't accelerate innovation; it just makes it easier to lock patients and providers into incompatible systems.

I'm not arguing that all standards are perfect or that the certification process couldn't be streamlined. But the absence of published outcome studies doesn't mean transparency requirements lack real-world benefit. These standards are too recent to have generated the kind of robust literature people might expect. What we do have are stories like my mother's—families wasting time, medication errors happening because information can't move between systems, and patients bearing the cost of vendor fragmentation.

If you're going to revise certification criteria, please don't start by removing the requirements that make systems transparent and accountable. Streamline the testing process if needed, but keep the disclosure mandates. Patient safety depends on it.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The commenter argues that healthcare AI transparency requirements—including disclosure of algorithmic decision-making, training data, and known limitations—are essential safeguards that should not be weakened in pursuit of faster interoperability,...
Comment:
Re: Request for Information – Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity

The Coalition for Health AI submits this comment on behalf of patients, families, and clinicians who depend on transparent, accountable health information systems. We appreciate the opportunity to engage with this important modernization effort.

My mother's Alzheimer's diagnosis last year exposed how fragmented our health IT systems are. But the three months we spent moving her records between her Seattle neurologist and a Spokane memory care facility taught me something else: speed without safeguards isn't efficiency. It's just faster chaos. When we finally got her complete file transferred, I had no way to know whether any of the recommended cognitive assessments came from the neurologist's clinical judgment or from some algorithm embedded in their EHR that neither the doctor nor we fully understood. That gap between what the system recommended and what my mother could actually consent to—that's what concerns me about these proposed changes.

Healthcare AI is already in use. Hospitals deploy risk stratification algorithms. Diagnostic support systems flag abnormalities. Treatment recommendations flow through machine learning models. Patients and clinicians have a fundamental right to know when a high-stakes health recommendation originates from an algorithmic system rather than human expert judgment, and what that system's actual limitations are—who it was tested on, what it might miss, what trade-offs its designers made.

We urge the agencies to preserve certification requirements that mandate detailed AI model transparency: intended use, target population, known failure modes, training data characteristics, and independent validation processes. These aren't burdens; they're the foundation of informed consent in healthcare. A developer can reduce burden by shipping better products with clearer documentation, not by reducing disclosure.

Regarding certification criteria revision: any changes should explicitly protect the transparency standards that allow clinicians and patients to understand when they're relying on algorithmic guidance. Interoperability matters. Speed matters. But neither should come at the cost of knowing what's actually driving a treatment decision.

—Andrew Torres
Campaign Director, Coalition for Health AI
Seattle, Washington

Comment:
Ainsley Advisory Group appreciates the opportunity to comment on this proposed deregulatory framework. We work directly with health IT developers, and I've watched firsthand what happens when compliance architecture becomes the primary competitive advantage rather than the product itself. Last year, a startup we advised—solid clinical validation, real interoperability gains—walked away from the market after calculating eighteen months and $2.3 million just to meet certification requirements that three incumbents had already absorbed into their cost structures years ago.

That said, we need to be careful about what we remove. Model cards and transparency standards for AI-driven tools aren't bureaucratic overhead. They're the mechanism that lets smaller developers compete on merit rather than just capital reserves, and they're what patients and clinicians actually need to make informed decisions about adopting new systems. When a smaller vendor can't afford to document how their algorithm behaves across different patient populations, or what its failure modes look like, they're not just facing a compliance burden—they're locked out of the conversation before they start.

On certification criteria specifically, we'd suggest examining whether existing interoperability standards like FHIR and SMART App Launch could serve as safe harbors, reducing the need for redundant testing where those standards are properly implemented. That's different from removing transparency requirements wholesale. The conditions of maintenance piece is where real relief could happen—the annual recertification cycles feel designed for legacy systems, not the continuous deployment model that modern developers actually use. But again, that's about streamlining process, not eliminating the substantive safeguards that level the playing field.

Our concern is that deregulation framed as burden reduction often just shifts the burden from compliance to market concentration. We'd rather see targeted relief that keeps the guardrails that smaller innovators depend on.

Comment:
Thank you for this notice and the opportunity to comment. I'm a postdoctoral fellow at the American College of Surgeons studying health IT safety and interoperability outcomes. I want to flag what I think is a significant gap in how the agency is framing the evidence question around certification criteria for AI transparency and detailed vendor disclosures.

Last month I watched my mother's cardiologist spend twenty minutes on hold trying to get her medication list from her rheumatologist because the two EHR systems don't talk to each other. She's been on the same three drugs for two years. When I asked why they couldn't just plug in an interoperability standard, he said smaller vendors have basically stopped trying because there's no regulatory incentive anymore. That conversation made me realize the research I'm designing on whether transparency requirements actually prevent harm is becoming harder to conduct, not easier, if we remove the mechanisms that force vendors to document their validation processes and known limitations.

The RFI suggests that because we don't yet have published outcome studies on AI model card transparency or detailed DSI certification disclosures, these requirements may lack real-world benefit. I need to say directly: the absence of published evidence on a recent requirement is not the same as evidence of absence. Our field moves slowly. Rigorous outcome studies on health IT interventions take three to five years from design to publication. The Cures Act certification rules on AI disclosures and risk documentation are still being operationalized. It's like claiming a medication doesn't work because we haven't finished the clinical trial yet.

My concern about removing detailed certification criteria for AI model documentation is simpler: once those documentation requirements go away, they're gone. Vendors won't voluntarily rebuild that transparency infrastructure. If I want to study whether knowing a model's training dataset, validation metrics, and known failure modes helps clinicians make better adoption decisions—which seems like a basic question we should answer before we eliminate the requirement—I won't be able to, because the data won't exist. No vendor will document what they're not required to document.

I'm not opposed to streamlining burden where it makes sense. But I'd ask the agency to distinguish between requirements that genuinely duplicate effort and requirements that generate information we simply haven't had time to study yet. The AI transparency criteria fall in the second category.

The Children's Hospital Association appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory framework for health IT certification and interoperability standards.

Our organization represents 170 children's hospitals across the United States, and we engage regularly with families navigating fragmented care delivery. Last year, we worked with a family whose child's oncology records were split across three health systems—initial diagnosis at one facility, chemotherapy management at another, follow-up care at a third. When they sought a second opinion, administrative delays and technical incompatibilities created a six-week gap in their decision-making timeline. That experience illustrates a real problem: patients need better access to their own data, and providers need clearer pathways to share it.

But I want to flag something specific about the proposed removals. If we eliminate AI model card transparency requirements—the documentation that explains how algorithmic tools perform, where they fail, and what clinicians should watch for—we're removing one of the few mechanisms that actually gets safety information into the hands of the people using these systems. Clinicians need to know what they're relying on. A pediatric oncologist using an algorithm to help interpret imaging needs to understand its limitations, its training data, its error rates in populations that matter to her patients. Without that transparency, we're trading interoperability gains for a kind of blind spot that could harm the very patients we're trying to serve.

I understand the burden argument. Developers want clearer rules and less compliance complexity. That's reasonable. But I'd encourage the agency to distinguish between transparency requirements that protect patient safety and administrative redundancies that don't. Model card documentation is the former. It's not about creating new barriers—it's about ensuring that the clinical teams using these tools have the information they need to use them responsibly.

Last month, one of our vendors told my team something we're still trying to work through. They said our EHR system can't meet both the new federal certification requirements and Alaska's stricter privacy rules for indigenous health data. We'd have to choose. Three weeks later, we're still stuck, and nobody at the federal level could give us a straight answer about whether we'd be breaking state law if we just went with the federal standard. Meanwhile, our public health reporting system sits in limbo because we're running 1990s-era data sharing protocols while we figure this out.

I understand the drive to reduce burden on vendors. But I'm concerned that cutting transparency requirements—specifically the detailed AI model cards under the DSI certification criterion—will make situations like ours worse, not better.

Here's what that criterion actually does: it requires vendors to document what their AI systems are for, who they're intended to serve, what risks they pose, and where the training data came from. That's not regulatory bloat. That's the floor. When a vendor tells you those things are incompatible with federal compliance, you need documentation to push back. You need something concrete. Right now we don't have it.

Alaska's indigenous health data protections aren't arbitrary federal overreach—they're a state legal obligation we take seriously. If we strip out the AI transparency requirements to ease federal certification, I'm betting we'll see more vendors telling us exactly what we heard last month: pick federal compliance or state law, not both.

I'm not asking for more regulation. I'm asking you not to remove the parts that actually let states and the federal government work together. The DSI criterion is what prevents this kind of vendor ultimatum. Loosen it, and you're not reducing burden on anyone except the companies pressuring us to choose.

Thank you for the opportunity to comment on this proposal. I'm writing from the perspective of someone who has already paid the price for rigorous standards, and I want to be direct: removing model card disclosure requirements won't level the playing field for smaller vendors like mine. It will tilt it further toward competitors with resources to hide their shortcuts.

Last year, HEP spent eighteen months and nearly $400,000 getting our pediatric EHR module certified. We did the work. We documented our AI design decisions, ran the validation studies, submitted everything. Then we watched a competitor with triple our resources launch a comparable product in four months using a different standards pathway. They cut corners. We didn't. And they won the contract.

What concerns me isn't compliance burden in the abstract—it's that inconsistent application of standards punishes companies that invested in doing things right. If this rule weakens AI transparency requirements without enforcing them uniformly across all vendors, we're not reducing burden. We're accelerating an unfair dynamic where only firms that can afford to obscure their AI design win contracts. Smaller players either dump another half-million into chasing whatever loopholes open up, or we accept that we can't compete fairly anymore.

Model card requirements are the only meaningful equalizer we have. They force disclosure of how algorithms work, what data trained them, what their failure modes are. For pediatric health IT, this isn't regulatory bloat—it's a patient safety requirement. Black-box AI in children's healthcare needs to be justifiable to clinicians, hospital systems, and families. Model cards are the bare minimum transparency needed to do that. Removing them signals that cost reduction matters more than accountability.

The real problem isn't that standards are too strict. The problem is they're applied inconsistently. One vendor gets eighteen months of scrutiny. Another gets four months and a different pathway to market. That's not innovation. That's regulatory arbitrage, and it rewards the competitors who can afford lawyers and compliance consultants to find the gaps.

If the goal is genuinely to reduce unnecessary burden on developers, then enforce standards consistently across the board. Don't eliminate transparency requirements—apply them the same way to every vendor, regardless of size or resources. That's what levels the field. That's what actually protects both patient safety and fair competition.

Right now I'm trying to decide whether to invest another $500,000 in chasing loopholes we don't even know about yet, or whether to tell my team we're looking at consolidation or exit. I don't want either option. What I want is a playing field where my investment in doing things right matters.

Carol Nguyen
Compliance Officer
HEP, LLC
Nevada

Thank you for the opportunity to comment on this Request for Information. I'm writing as a practice manager who has spent the last eighteen months learning why transparency requirements matter—the hard way.

Last year, our three-person development team spent six months and thirty thousand dollars getting certified for a feature we believed would streamline our hospital's workflow around AI-assisted diagnostics. We followed the rules. We built model cards. We documented performance across patient populations. And then the market didn't want it. That's real money from a real small shop. But I've watched what happens when you remove transparency requirements. You don't level the field. You tilt it harder toward whoever can afford to push untested tools into the market and absorb the liability when things go wrong. Our hospital's procurement team can't evaluate an AI tool without a detailed model card—we don't have a data science staff, we have clinicians and workflow managers trying to figure out fit for our specific patient population. Strip that requirement and we're not choosing based on evidence anymore. We're choosing based on vendor marketing claims and whoever's sales rep is best at golf.

The DSI model card disclosure is not a burden. It's the only honest way to compare tools. When we're evaluating a diagnostic AI, I need to know: how was this trained, what's the false positive rate in patients over 65, does it work in our rural Colorado setting where our patient demographics are different from whatever academic medical center trained the algorithm. Without that, I'm flying blind. A larger vendor can hire someone to write marketing materials that make untested tools sound revolutionary. I can't. So deregulation doesn't help shops like mine compete. It just rewards the vendors who cut corners on transparency and punishes the ones who did the work.

I'm not asking for more rules. I'm asking the RFI to resist the pressure to remove disclosure requirements in the name of reducing burden. The burden isn't the transparency—it's the instability. Rules that keep getting rewritten to favor whoever lobbies hardest make it impossible to plan. We invested in compliance because we believed it was the right thing and the stable thing. Now I'm watching that foundation erode. If you want smaller developers to succeed, give us regulatory stability and make sure the big players can't just skip the work we already paid for.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The Dental Standards Institute urges retention of AI model card disclosure requirements in the proposed deregulatory framework, arguing that while some streamlining of compliance processes is appropriate, eliminating transparency mechanisms for AI...
Comment:
The Dental Standards Institute appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory framework under the Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability initiative. Our organization has spent the better part of two decades working with dental practices, health systems, and technology vendors to establish meaningful standards for clinical data management and AI integration in oral healthcare. We represent over 180 member organizations across Minnesota, Wisconsin, and the broader Upper Midwest, and our policy work focuses specifically on how regulatory frameworks either enable or impede the safe adoption of health information technology in dental settings.

I want to be direct about why we are submitting this comment: last year, one of our member clinics in Duluth experienced a data breach that crystallized for us what happens when transparency requirements are weakened in pursuit of deregulation. A vendor with access to patient records—access granted through what appeared to be routine business associate arrangements—exploited a gap in the clinic's data governance system and accessed complete treatment histories, including sensitive notes about a patient's substance abuse recovery. The breach notification process worked as intended, technically speaking. What didn't work was the accountability mechanism. The vendor faced no meaningful enforcement action, and because the data had been "anonymized," there was no clear legal basis to prevent them from selling derivative datasets to competing practices. This experience has shaped how we think about what deregulation actually means in practice.

The proposed rule raises genuine concerns about how far the pendulum should swing in reducing certification requirements, particularly those relating to AI model transparency. We want to be careful here—we are not arguing against reducing burden on developers or streamlining compliance processes where that makes sense. The dental IT market is smaller and more fragmented than general healthcare IT, and we recognize that overly prescriptive standards can slow innovation and create barriers for smaller vendors. But we also think there is a significant difference between reducing administrative burden and removing transparency mechanisms that serve patient safety and clinical accountability.

Specifically, we urge the agency to retain the current DSI certification criterion requiring detailed AI model card disclosures. This criterion requires vendors to document intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and validation processes before certification. We know this adds documentation work. We also know it is foundational to patient safety, clinical adoption, and accountability in healthcare AI applications, and we think the deregulatory impulse has not adequately reckoned with what gets lost when these requirements disappear.

Here is why this matters concretely. A dental practice implementing an AI-assisted diagnostic tool needs to know whether that tool was trained on imaging data that included diverse patient populations or whether it was predominantly trained on scans from patients with particular demographic characteristics. This is not a technical nicety. The literature on algorithmic bias in medical imaging is substantial, and dentistry is not immune to these issues. When a practice adopts a tool without understanding its training data composition, they are assuming blind risk. The model card requirement gives the practice—and the patient—a basis for understanding what that tool can and cannot reliably do. Without it, the practice is operating on faith in the vendor's assertions.

The second piece is clinical accountability. When something goes wrong—when a tool produces a false negative and a patient's oral cancer is missed, or when a diagnostic recommendation is systematically biased against certain patient populations—there needs to be a clear mechanism for understanding how the tool was built and validated. The model card documentation creates that mechanism. It is not a perfect safeguard, but it is the baseline transparency required for meaningful accountability. A practice cannot investigate what went wrong if the vendor never disclosed what the tool was designed to do, what data it was trained on, or what its known limitations are.

We have also considered the vendor perspective, and we think some of the concerns about burden are legitimate. Smaller companies, in particular, may struggle with the administrative cost of producing comprehensive model card documentation. But the solution to this problem is not to eliminate the requirement—it is to streamline the process, provide clearer templates, or consider scaled requirements based on vendor size and deployment scope. The FDA has done this in other contexts. The requirement itself is not the burden; the vagueness of the requirement is. Clear, standardized documentation is actually easier to produce than ambiguous, ad hoc disclosures.

What also concerns us is the broader trajectory. The proposed rule frames certification requirements as obstacles to innovation and market entry. In some cases, this framing is fair. But certification requirements exist because healthcare is not a normal market. Patients cannot easily switch providers to punish bad vendors. The information asymmetry between vendors and clinical users is profound. Network effects lock practices into particular vendors and their tools. In this context, upfront transparency requirements are not anti-competitive obstacles—they are the market mechanism that allows clinicians to make informed choices. Shouldn't the doctors using it know what it was tested on, what its limits are, where it might fail?

Removing transparency requirements—making it so clinicians can't see how an algorithm was built or validated—that's the opposite of solving my problem. That's hiding information instead of sharing it. I want interoperability so my husband's records move seamlessly. I absolutely want that. But not if it means hospitals are deploying black-box systems nobody fully understands. That's just a different kind of delay. We need both: data that flows freely AND visibility into the tools making decisions on top of that data.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: Deregulation efforts that eliminate transparency requirements like Developer System Information disclosures shift due diligence burdens onto health systems rather than reducing overall burden, and faster data exchange can be achieved without gutti...
Comment:
William Martin, Policy Director
CareQuest Institute for Oral Health
Austin, Texas

RE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity (RFI)

The tension I encounter most often in our work isn't between innovation and regulation—it's between two legitimate but contradictory pressures: the need for faster data exchange and the responsibility to understand what tools we're actually deploying in clinical settings. Last year, we lost a patient to periodontal disease progression that might have been preventable if she could have moved her records between practices within weeks instead of months. The system locked her data for liability reasons. But when I brought the problem to our board, our compliance officers pointed out that loosening those restrictions without transparency about *how* the data would be used and *what algorithms* would interpret it could create different liabilities—ones we couldn't quantify because we had no visibility into vendor systems.

That real-world bind is what concerns me about the direction of this deregulation effort.

CareQuest has helped dental and health systems across 20 states evaluate digital health tools. What I've learned is that hospital procurement teams—the people who actually decide whether to deploy an algorithm or interoperability standard—are operating blind when transparency requirements vanish. They don't need less regulation. They need better information to make responsible decisions. When you strip away Developer System Information model card disclosures in the name of reducing burden, you don't eliminate the burden. You shift it onto health systems that now must conduct expensive, parallel due diligence on their own, hire consultants to reverse-engineer vendor tools, or worse, adopt systems they don't fully understand because they lack the leverage to demand answers.

I'm not opposed to streamlining certification criteria or reducing friction in standards development. Those are separate from transparency. USCDI interoperability standards and DSI disclosure requirements serve different functions. You can make data exchange faster without gutting the disclosure infrastructure that lets clinicians and procurement teams know what they're buying. What worries me is that the RFI conflates those two things.

The specific question about revision to maintenance of certification requirements: I'd push back on removing reporting requirements tied to actual clinical deployment. Keep the certification thresholds lean, sure. But health systems need model cards that describe *what the tool was trained on*, *what populations it's been tested in*, and *what performance degradation looks like outside those populations*. That's not theater. That's the baseline accountability structure that lets us answer to our patients.

Comment:
March 15, 2025

Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology
U.S. Department of Health and Human Services
330 C Street SW
Washington, DC 20201

Re: Public Comment on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions

Dear HHS Leadership:

The Federation of State Medical Boards writes to express our strong support for efforts to streamline health information exchange and reduce unnecessary regulatory burden on health IT developers and providers. We recognize the Office of the National Coordinator's commitment to enabling innovation and competition in the health technology market, and we share the goal of making it easier for patients and providers to access and move health information across systems. That said, we have a specific concern about one proposed deregulatory action that we believe warrants careful reconsideration: the removal of the Data Segmentation for Privacy (DSI) certification criterion that currently requires developers of health IT systems to disclose detailed information about artificial intelligence models used in clinical decision support and other high-stakes healthcare applications.

Before explaining our position, I should note that this concern arose from a real situation that affected one of our board members' patients. Last year, an elderly woman switched cardiologists after moving closer to her family. The previous practice could not quickly transfer her records in a usable format—they claimed the systems simply wouldn't export the data in a way the new practice could import. The new cardiologist had to order three months of diagnostic testing from scratch. The patient paid thousands out of pocket, and her treatment plan was delayed by weeks. Everyone involved was frustrated, and frankly, angry. When we learned that the proposed rules might make record transfers faster and easier, we thought finally, something is going to fix this problem. But then we started reading more carefully about what deregulation might mean, and we realized that speed without security, and access without accountability, could create different kinds of harm.

This brings us to the AI model card requirement under the DSI criterion. We understand the appeal of removing this requirement. Developers have told us it adds cost and complexity. The certification process takes time. There are legitimate concerns about burden. But we believe the current requirement serves a critical function that shouldn't be discarded lightly, and we want to explain why from the perspective of state medical boards who oversee physician practice.

**Why AI Transparency Matters in Healthcare**

State medical boards regulate physicians and are responsible for protecting the public from incompetent or unsafe practice. When a physician uses a health IT system that incorporates AI—whether it's a diagnostic support tool, a clinical workflow recommendation system, or a predictive analytics application—the physician is responsible for understanding how that tool works and whether it's appropriate for their patient. But here's the problem: most physicians cannot independently verify whether an AI model is safe, accurate, or suitable for their specific patient population. They lack the technical expertise, and they don't have access to the training data, validation studies, or known limitations of the models.

This creates a transparency gap. A cardiologist in Massachusetts cannot know whether a diagnostic recommendation system was trained on data that adequately represents elderly women with complex comorbidities, or whether the model performs equally well across different racial and ethnic groups, or whether it has known failure modes in certain clinical contexts. Without that information, the physician is essentially using a black box. They may believe they're practicing evidence-based medicine when they're actually relying on a system whose performance characteristics are unknown to them.

The current DSI certification criterion requires developers to disclose, in plain language, the intended use of AI models, the target population for which they were designed, known risks and limitations, characteristics of the training data, and the validation processes used. This isn't an onerous requirement. It's asking developers to write down what they should already know about their own products. And it's asking them to make that information available to the physicians and healthcare systems that use those products.

When we talk to physicians about this, they tell us they want this information. They don't want to be surprised by limitations that could affect patient care. They want to know if a model was validated in a diverse population or only in a narrow subset. They want to know if there are known accuracy problems in certain scenarios. They want to understand the reasoning behind the system's recommendations well enough to explain it to patients. These aren't unreasonable asks.

**The Connection to Patient Safety and Clinical Adoption**

There's also a practical question of clinical adoption. Right now, many physicians are skeptical of AI-based clinical decision support tools. Some of that skepticism is warranted—there have been well-publicized failures and biases in healthcare AI systems. But some of it is simply uncertainty. Physicians don't know whether to trust a system because they can't see inside it. If we

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: Removing AI model card disclosure and certification requirements would recreate vendor lock-in problems by eliminating transparency mechanisms that allow clinicians and smaller health systems to assess algorithmic bias and performance, ultimately ...
Organization Name: NORC at the University of Chicago
Comment:
Over the past year, I've analyzed claims data from three major health systems in the tri-state area looking at care coordination for patients with complex chronic conditions. What I found was systematic delays in treatment initiation and medication reconciliation—not because the technology didn't exist, but because the vendors' electronic systems were designed not to talk to each other. Interoperability would have been economically rational from a clinical standpoint. From a business standpoint, it threatened their market position. That's the vendor lock-in problem that certification standards were meant to address.

A colleague mentioned his mother had a medication interaction between her cardiologist and rheumatologist that might have been caught if their records had actually synchronized. She's one of thousands. This isn't abstract for me anymore.

I want to be direct about what concerns me with the current proposal. The request to remove AI model card disclosure requirements and revise certification criteria sounds efficiency-minded in principle. In practice, it eliminates the empirical foundation that health economists, clinicians, and smaller health systems need to assess whether algorithmic tools are actually reducing costs and improving outcomes, or just shifting risk to vulnerable populations. Without transparency requirements, vendors have no incentive to disclose algorithmic bias, cost effectiveness data, or population-specific performance metrics. Smaller safety-net providers and community health systems can't conduct due diligence on vendor claims. We end up back where we started—with information asymmetries that favor large vendors and lock out competition.

I've read the economic case for deregulation. It assumes perfect information and competitive markets. Health IT markets are neither. The certification criteria for AI-based decision support tools function as a transparency mechanism that levels an uneven playing field. Remove them, and information rents increase for incumbents. Clinicians lose the ability to detect systematic algorithmic bias before it scales across populations.

I'm not arguing against innovation. I'm arguing that transparency is pro-competition. Model card disclosures don't slow down good vendors. They slow down bad ones. If you want to reduce burden on developers, I'd suggest narrowing the scope of required disclosures rather than eliminating them—specify which performance metrics matter most for different use cases. But don't remove the requirement to disclose. That repeats the mistake we made with interoperability standards in the first place.

Comment:
Verve, LLC appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed modifications to health IT certification criteria. As a healthcare administrator managing EHR vendor relationships across multiple facilities in Texas, I've seen firsthand how certification barriers can freeze innovation in place. Last year we evaluated a smaller vendor whose interoperability approach was genuinely superior to what the market leaders offered, but their incomplete certification suite made them too risky for our compliance team to adopt. We defaulted to our existing vendors instead.

That said, I want to be direct about one area where I think the current DSI certification requirement for detailed AI model card disclosures should remain unchanged. The requirement asking for intended use specifications, target population definitions, known risks, training data characteristics, and validation processes isn't bureaucratic overhead—it's foundational information that clinicians actually need to make safe decisions about where and how to deploy these tools in patient care. When an AI model is trained on a particular demographic or validated only under specific conditions, that matters. Our clinical teams need to know those constraints before they rely on the system.

I understand the impulse to lower barriers for smaller vendors. Competition would genuinely serve us better. But model transparency isn't the barrier that's keeping good companies out of the market. The issue is the total cost and timeline of the full certification suite, not the depth of documentation required for tools that influence clinical decisions. Removing the model card requirement wouldn't meaningfully accelerate market entry for the startup we looked at—they needed the full certification path anyway. What would help is a tiered pathway where lower-risk functionality gets certified faster, while higher-stakes AI applications face the transparency requirements they should face.

I'd urge the agency to distinguish between compliance burdens that genuinely slow innovation without safety benefit, and transparency requirements that actually protect patient safety and enable clinical adoption. The first group should be streamlined. The second should stay intact.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The Digital Medicine Society urges regulators to retain AI model card disclosure requirements in health IT certification, arguing that transparency about algorithmic intended use, limitations, and validation processes is essential for patient safe...
Comment:
RE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions

The Digital Medicine Society appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed changes to health IT certification requirements. Our organization represents patient advocates, community health workers, and affected families across the Midwest who have experienced firsthand the consequences of fragmented health data systems.

Last year, my mother suffered a stroke in Omaha and was transferred between three hospitals in two weeks. Each transfer meant her medical records went missing—new physicians ordered duplicate imaging because they couldn't access previous scans. I spent days calling around to reconstruct her medication list. What struck me most, though, was receiving unsolicited calls from data brokers within weeks of her hospitalization, asking to purchase her health information. That's when I realized how broken the system is on both sides: clinicians can't access what they need to treat patients safely, while companies can easily acquire and monetize our most sensitive information.

We urge the agency to retain the current DSI certification criterion requiring detailed AI model card disclosures—including intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and validation processes. This isn't bureaucratic bloat. It's foundational to patient safety.

Here's why this matters practically: when algorithmic tools are deployed in clinical settings without transparent documentation of what they were designed to do and who they were tested on, patients become unwitting test subjects. If a sepsis prediction model was trained primarily on adult data but gets used in pediatric wards, clinicians won't know it's operating outside its validated population. If a diagnostic AI has known blind spots for certain demographics—something we're seeing repeatedly in cardiovascular and kidney disease algorithms—that needs to be documented and visible to the people using it.

Removing these transparency requirements won't speed up innovation. It will speed up adoption of tools that haven't been properly vetted. Clinicians already struggle with alert fatigue and tool proliferation; they need trustworthy information about what each system actually does, not more black boxes.

We also saw during the pandemic how quickly "temporary" data access arrangements became permanent, and how hard it was to know where our information went. Streamlining interoperability is important. Eliminating accountability around what gets disclosed is not.

We support reducing unnecessary compliance burden where it makes sense. But certification criteria that require vendors to document their AI models' intended use, limitations, and validation processes protect patients and ultimately support clinical adoption. Providers will trust and use tools they understand.

Please retain this requirement. We're happy to discuss implementation details that could ease compliance without sacrificing transparency.

Respectfully,

David Lee
Community Organizer
Digital Medicine Society
Omaha, Nebraska

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: CareAtlas argues that removing AI model card and DSI disclosure requirements would harm competition by eliminating standardized transparency on algorithmic performance, forcing hospitals to conduct redundant private due diligence instead and perpe...
Comment:
CareAtlas Inc. appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory framework. As a hospital system managing complex vendor relationships and substantial EHR investments across Maryland, we have direct experience with how certification requirements shape market dynamics—and why transparency standards matter more than we tend to acknowledge.

Last year we conducted a serious evaluation of a smaller EHR vendor whose maternal-fetal medicine module genuinely outperformed our incumbent system. The clinical team preferred it. The difference in usability was measurable. But our compliance team spent eight months on due diligence specifically around ONC certification gaps. That vendor still couldn't clear the bar. We walked away from what was probably the better product because the switching costs—not the product itself, but the regulatory uncertainty around an uncertified system—made the board uncomfortable. Today, two larger competitors with established certification pathways have already moved into that clinical space with inferior solutions. The smaller vendor is still outside the door.

I mention this because the current proposal seems to assume that certification burden is purely procedural, when what we actually experience is that fragmented or opaque requirements lock in incumbent vendors by making switching costs prohibitive. That's consolidation, not competition.

Here's what concerns me about removing AI model card and DSI disclosure requirements specifically: we don't have enough information about algorithm performance across our vendor portfolio now, and we need more, not less. When I evaluate whether to incur the switching costs for a new system, I need validated performance data on how that vendor's AI handles our patient population—not marketing claims, not generic benchmarks, but disclosure about the models actually running in our system. Without that, I'm flying blind. I can't build a business case for switching because I can't quantify the risk differential.

Model cards aren't process burden. They're data transparency. Streamlining how vendors format and submit those disclosures makes sense. Eliminating what they must disclose doesn't solve the problem—it just shifts the risk onto hospital CFOs and compliance teams who end up doing ad hoc due diligence anyway, except now we're doing it privately, redundantly, and less rigorously than a standardized disclosure would allow.

We support simplifying certification workflows, reducing redundant documentation, and clearing away genuinely unnecessary process steps. But transparency about algorithmic performance isn't unnecessary overhead. It's what lets smaller vendors compete on merit and what lets hospitals like ours actually switch when better solutions exist.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050

Comment:
Verve, LLC respectfully submits this comment on the proposed deregulatory actions affecting health IT certification and interoperability standards. We support the underlying goal of reducing unnecessary burden on developers, but want to flag a critical gap in the current reform approach: the risk of stripping away transparency requirements that actually enable both innovation and safe adoption.

Last fall, I worked with a health tech startup in Casper founded by two nurses. They'd identified a genuine workflow problem in patient discharge—something hospital staff had been flagging for years. The product was sound. What killed their launch timeline wasn't the certification process itself; it was uncertainty about what certification would require. They faced eighteen months of delays trying to understand fragmented requirements from different health systems, each with different risk profiles and patient populations. That ambiguity created hidden compliance costs far larger than a clear, standardized pathway would have.

Here's what struck me: when I finally helped them map out a predictable certification framework with explicit model card disclosure requirements—training data, intended use population, known performance gaps, training methodologies—the cost picture changed entirely. They could plan resources. They could budget for it. More importantly, hospital procurement teams could actually evaluate whether the tool fit their specific populations and risk tolerances. That standardization became a competitive advantage, not a burden.

The RFI asks which certification criteria should be removed to reduce burden while maintaining interoperability. Our recommendation is this: preserve and clarify the model card disclosure requirements, but streamline the submission pathways for vendors meeting those standards. The transparency itself isn't the friction point. The friction is opaque, inconsistent requirements that force vendors to guess and health systems to negotiate individually.

Removing model card disclosures won't reduce costs for smaller developers—it will shift the burden to every hospital procurement team trying to make adoption decisions. Right now, hospital CIOs and patient safety officers depend on documented information about training data, population fit, and known risks. That information is how they decide whether to pilot a tool or reject it. Without it, procurement becomes either reckless or impossibly slow.

We'd also note that smaller health tech innovators actually benefit from regulatory clarity. What burdens them is fragmentation. The Casper startup could have moved eighteen months faster if certification criteria had been explicit and standardized from the beginning. Deregulation should mean clearer rules, not fewer rules.

We support streamlining. We urge you to preserve the transparency guardrails that let smaller vendors plan with confidence and hospital teams make informed decisions.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: Healthcare institutions are exploiting regulatory uncertainty to avoid transparency requirements—three member hospitals shared patient data without consent because ambiguous enforcement made compliance feel optional—and weakening DSI certification...
Comment:
The Healthcare Leadership Council represents 20 health systems and major teaching hospitals across 14 states. What I want to say plainly is this: last spring, we discovered that three of our member hospitals had been sharing patient data with third-party analytics firms without explicit consent. Our legal team told us they did this because the enforcement environment had become so uncertain that compliance felt optional. That's not a theoretical concern. It happened.

I understand the agency's push to reduce burden on developers. I do. But there's a real difference between eliminating genuinely redundant requirements and stripping away the transparency rules that prevent institutions from taking shortcuts they know they shouldn't take. The RFI suggests that model card and DSI certification criteria lack empirical support, and I need to push back on that framing. Transparency requirements are too recent to have generated the peer-reviewed longitudinal literature the agency seems to be demanding. That doesn't mean they lack evidence of benefit. It means we can't judge a nascent safeguard by the publication timeline of mature fields. What we can judge is what happens when those safeguards are absent—and we're already seeing it. Hospitals are gambling because uncertainty has made compliance negotiable.

Regarding your question about which certification criteria should be revised: removing transparency mandates is not a burden reduction. It's a compliance erosion accelerator. When you weaken DSI standards for AI model transparency, you send a message to the field that these guardrails are negotiable. The hospitals in our systems that shared patient data without consent weren't operating in a vacuum. They were responding to signals that enforcement was uneven and ambiguous. Tightening standards doesn't impose gratuitous burden—it establishes the baseline expectation that institutions cannot operate in darkness and call it innovation.

What I would ask instead is whether there are ways to streamline the implementation pathway for transparency requirements without gutting the requirements themselves. Can developers meet certification criteria more efficiently? Can the approval process move faster? Those are legitimate burden questions. Removing the criteria altogether just creates the conditions for more institutions to do what our member hospitals did.

We need guardrails. Real ones. Not the appearance of guardrails.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: A public health official argues that removing AI transparency requirements from clinical certification standards creates dangerous accountability gaps similar to those that delayed outbreak response during a hepatitis A crisis, and that vendors' b...
Comment:
Last month we had a hepatitis A outbreak here in the city. Three cases turned into five, five into nine. The whole thing could have been contained faster, but one of our clinic partners was using an EHR system from a vendor who claimed they didn't have to share outbreak data with us because their certification status didn't require interoperability at that particular level. I spent two days on the phone trying to figure out who actually enforces what. By the time we got the data we needed, two more people were already hospitalized.

I've been doing public health work for twelve years. I've seen vendors push back on requirements before. But what happened during that outbreak wasn't about whether the rules were perfect—it was about basic visibility. We couldn't see what data existed, we couldn't force anyone to explain how their system actually worked, and we couldn't move fast when lives depended on it.

Now I'm reading about proposals to strip out AI model transparency requirements from certification criteria. I get that vendors want less red tape. Innovation matters. But I need to be direct: removing disclosure requirements for how AI systems make decisions in clinical settings is the same vendor accountability vacuum that nearly cost us during that outbreak, just dressed up differently.

Here's what concerns me most. If a vendor's AI system is recommending isolation protocols or flagging patients at risk for communicable disease, and I can't see how that algorithm actually works—can't see what data it's trained on, what it's optimized for, where it breaks down—then I'm being asked to oversee something I can't inspect. That's not innovation. That's opacity masquerading as efficiency.

The burden argument doesn't hold up when you ask public health departments to manage the fallout. A model card disclosure costs a vendor time. A system failure costs us lives.

I'm not asking for perfection in every certification criterion. But pulling back on AI transparency now, before widespread deployment, is betting that we'll catch the problems later through documented harm. I've lived that bet. It loses.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: A health system policy analyst argues that removing AI model card disclosure requirements from certification criteria would be a mistake, as vendors' refusal to disclose training data, validation processes, and known failure modes prevents health ...
Organization Name: MCG Health, part of the Hearst Health network
Comment:
To the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology:

I work as a senior policy analyst at MCG Health, part of the Hearst Health network in Massachusetts. We're a mid-sized health system with multiple hospital campuses and primary care practices, so we interact with health IT vendors constantly—both the large EHR vendors and smaller third-party developers trying to build tools for patient engagement and care coordination. What I'm going to say here comes from actually trying to make these systems work in practice, not from some abstract policy perspective.

Last month we discovered that our primary EHR vendor had been throttling API requests from a patient's third-party health app without our knowledge. They claimed it was a "security concern," but when we dug into it, it became clear they were using API throttling as contractual leverage to push us toward their own competing apps. When we tried to audit the actual data flows—to verify that patient information was being properly protected during transfer—we hit a wall. The vendor's APIs are essentially black boxes. We couldn't see the training data characteristics, the validation processes, the known failure modes, anything that would let us assess whether the system was actually safe. And we're the paying customer.

That experience made me pay close attention to the proposal to remove the DSI certification criterion requiring detailed AI model card disclosures. I need to be direct: I think that would be a mistake, and I want to explain why from the perspective of someone actually trying to implement this technology in a health system.

The current certification requirement asks vendors to disclose intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and validation processes. This sounds like it's about transparency for transparency's sake. But it's not. It's about accountability in systems that directly affect patient safety. And I've learned that the hard way.

Here's the reality: when a vendor refuses to disclose how their AI model was trained, what populations it was validated on, or what its known failure modes are, you can't make an informed decision about whether to use it. You can't audit it. You can't know whether it's safe for your patient population. At MCG Health, we serve a lot of patients with complex comorbidities—our patient population skews older than national averages, with higher rates of chronic kidney disease and diabetes. If a vendor's AI model was trained primarily on younger, healthier populations, we need to know that. We need to know the model's performance degraded when applied to patients with certain characteristics. Without that information, we're essentially flying blind.

The argument I'm hearing from the deregulation side is that certification requirements create burden on vendors, slow down innovation, and that market competition will naturally weed out unsafe products. I understand that logic. And I'm not opposed to reducing unnecessary regulatory burden. But there's a category difference between a requirement that's genuinely unnecessary and one that's essential for informed decision-making. Model card disclosures are the latter.

When I look at what happened with our EHR vendor and the API throttling, the underlying problem wasn't that there was too much regulation. The problem was that we had no visibility into what they were doing. We couldn't verify claims about security. We couldn't compare their system to alternatives because we couldn't actually see inside their systems. And because we're locked into a multiyear contract with significant switching costs, we had very little leverage to demand better. In that context, certification criteria that require transparency aren't about creating burden—they're about creating a level playing field where health systems can actually make informed choices.

I also want to push back on the assumption that market competition alone will solve this. Healthcare technology markets aren't perfectly competitive. There are high switching costs. There's significant vendor lock-in. And there's information asymmetry—vendors know far more about their products than purchasers do. In that environment, certification criteria that mandate transparency aren't anti-market. They're pro-market. They level the information playing field so that health systems can actually make the choices that the theory of competitive markets assumes we can make.

There's another piece of this that matters for patient safety. When we implement an AI system in a clinical workflow, we need to understand its failure modes. We need to know: what happens when the model encounters data it wasn't trained on? What's the performance on edge cases? What are the documented differences in accuracy across demographic groups? These aren't academic questions. They're practical questions that affect patient care. If a vendor won't disclose this information, we have to assume either they don't know it—which is a problem—or they know it and don't want us to know—which is also a problem. Either way, requiring model card disclosures forces vendors to actually understand their own systems and be honest about what they do and don't do well.
