Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The commenter supports improved medical record accessibility but opposes weakening transparency and certification standards for AI systems and data handling, arguing that the lack of current evidence for these protections does not justify removing...
Comment:
My daughter's experience last year is exactly why I'm worried about this. When her insurance changed, the new doctor couldn't access her records for three months. We had to pay out of pocket for tests that were already done somewhere else. It was frustrating and expensive, and I get that making it easier to move records between doctors would help families like mine.

But here's what concerns me: the regulators are saying there's no proof that transparency requirements actually help patients. That doesn't make sense to me. These rules are brand new. Of course there aren't studies showing they work yet—nobody's had time to measure it. That's not the same as saying they don't help.

When doctors can't see how an AI system was trained or what it's supposed to do, that's when mistakes happen. That's when patients get wrong diagnoses. And when companies don't have to meet certain standards for handling data, that's when people's private medical information gets at risk. My daughter's health records shouldn't be floating around unsecured just because it's easier for tech companies that way.

I'm not against making things simpler. But simpler for who? If removing certification standards mostly helps the companies building these systems and puts the burden on patients like us to figure out if our data is safe, that's the wrong trade-off. Don't cut corners on transparency and safety just because it sounds like innovation.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: Healthcare providers and patients need transparency about how AI systems work and what data trains them because information gaps between specialists already cause dangerous medical errors, and removing transparency safeguards before their effectiv...
Comment:
My daughter got really sick last year and had to see three different specialists at the same hospital system. Every single visit, I filled out her medical history again, answered the same questions about her medications, her allergies—everything. One doctor didn't have notes from the previous visit, so we had to explain her whole situation from scratch. It was exhausting, and honestly, it scared me. What if they'd missed something? What if a medication interaction got overlooked because the information wasn't flowing between them?

I understand companies want less burden, but I have to say—when it comes to how AI systems work and what data they're trained on, transparency isn't a luxury. It's basic patient safety. We don't wait for ten years of outcome studies before we put warnings on medication bottles or make sure patients understand their treatment options. Those safeguards exist because we learned the hard way that people need information to stay safe. AI in healthcare should be the same.

The agency keeps saying there aren't published studies proving transparency helps patients. But that's different from proving it doesn't help. These requirements are still new. We're not talking about removing something proven useless—we're talking about removing guardrails before we even know if they're working.

As someone who works in home health, I see how confusion about medications and medical information affects my patients every day. Older people especially need clarity. Before we roll back what little transparency we have, we should be making sure doctors and families actually understand how these tools work.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The CareQuest Institute for Oral Health opposes removing AI model transparency requirements from DSI certification, arguing that detailed model cards disclosing intended use, target populations, risks, and validation data are essential safeguards ...
Comment:
RE: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity

The CareQuest Institute for Oral Health appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory framework. We represent a national network of dental health centers and community health workers serving vulnerable populations, and we want to raise serious concerns about removing AI model transparency requirements from the DSI certification criterion.

Last year, one of our clients needed dental records transferred between providers. It took three months and multiple phone calls just to get copies—meanwhile her new dentist couldn't start necessary treatment. That experience taught me something important: when systems fail to be transparent about how they work, patients pay the price. If we're now going to make it easier for dental and health systems to pull data without requiring detailed AI model cards—information about intended use, target populations, known risks, training data, and validation processes—we're essentially asking vulnerable patients to trust systems they can't understand or verify.

I understand the appeal of deregulation. Fewer rules mean faster development and lower costs for providers. But there's a real difference between reducing unnecessary burden and removing safeguards that actually matter. AI models in healthcare make consequential decisions about patient care. If a model was trained on data that doesn't represent diverse patient populations, or if it has known failure modes nobody disclosed, patients in our communities—who are already experiencing health disparities—will bear that risk disproportionately.

The model card requirement isn't bureaucratic theater. It's the foundation for clinical adoption, accountability, and patient safety. Providers need to know what they're using. Patients deserve to know if the system making decisions about their care was validated on people like them. And when something goes wrong, we need a clear record of what was tested and what wasn't.

We urge you to retain the current DSI certification criterion requiring detailed AI model card disclosures. Streamline other requirements if you must, but keep transparency where it protects patients. The goal should be smart deregulation, not blind deregulation.

Sharon Hall
Community Organizer
CareQuest Institute for Oral Health
New York

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: A healthcare IT vendor argues for maintaining detailed model card requirements in AI certification standards, contending that transparency about tool limitations and training data is essential for clinician understanding and patient safety, even t...
Comment:
Thank you for the opportunity to comment on these proposed changes to health IT certification standards. I want to be direct about my concern: we need to keep the DSI model card requirement intact, and I say this as someone who's been burned by regulatory whiplash.

Last year, Regard Technologies invested eighteen months and nearly $400,000 getting our patient scheduling module certified under the current standards. We documented everything—intended use cases, target populations, known limitations, training data characteristics, validation methodology. It was thorough and expensive. Then we watched a competitor launch something functionally similar in six weeks, cutting corners we thought were mandatory. When that happens, you start questioning whether you did the right thing.

But here's what I've learned in healthcare IT: the temptation to lighten the load always feels good until a patient gets harmed. Model card transparency isn't bureaucratic theater. It's the difference between clinicians understanding what a tool can and cannot do, and clinicians trusting something they don't actually understand. In high-stakes healthcare applications, that gap kills people.

The model card requirement—detailed disclosure of intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and validation processes—serves a function that's hard to see until it's missing. When a hospital implements an AI-assisted diagnostic tool, their clinical leadership needs to know what populations that tool was validated on. If it was trained primarily on data from academic medical centers and they're deploying it in a rural critical access hospital, they need to see that mismatch in writing. They need the model card to say it. Without it, they're flying blind.

I understand the argument about faster time-to-market and lower compliance costs. Believe me, I do. But certification standards exist because the alternative—the Wild West approach where vendors self-certify or claim their tools are "general purpose" and therefore unregulated—has a track record. We've seen it in other industries. And we're seeing early warning signs in healthcare AI right now.

What troubles me is that if we remove this requirement, we're not actually removing burden equally. Large vendors like us have compliance infrastructure and legal teams. We can absorb regulatory uncertainty. Smaller developers might compete on price by cutting corners on transparency. Hospitals might choose based on cost, not on how well they actually understand what they're buying. That's not innovation—that's a race to the bottom.

The eighteen months and $400,000 we spent? I'm not asking for pity. But I am saying it created something valuable: it forced us to truly understand our own tool's limitations. That exercise made us better. The model card we produced isn't just a checkbox for regulators. Our sales team uses it. Our support team uses it. Customers ask for it. Removing the requirement doesn't remove the need—it just removes the assurance that everyone's doing it.

I'd propose a middle path. Keep the DSI model card requirement for any AI-assisted clinical decision support or diagnostic tools. Consider streamlining it for lower-risk applications like scheduling or billing. But for anything that touches patient care decisions, the transparency has to stay. Certification should mean something. It should mean a clinician can read a document and know what they're implementing.

We'll comply with whatever standards you set. But please don't set them lower because we're tired of compliance. We're tired of compliance partly because we do it right.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: While supporting efforts to reduce unnecessary regulatory burden on health IT developers, the Digital Medicine Society cautions that removing certification requirements for model transparency and documentation would shift risk from vendors to hosp...
Comment:
Thomas Ramirez
Digital Medicine Society
Montgomery, Alabama

Re: Request for Information – Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions

The Digital Medicine Society supports the agency's interest in reducing unnecessary regulatory burden on health IT developers. We also recognize that innovation matters—hospitals need better tools, and we can't get there if every small vendor is crushed under compliance costs. That said, I want to flag something I keep thinking about from our health system procurement side: the burden we're talking about removing often lands on institutions and patients, not developers, and that's where I get concerned.

Last year our hospital's IT team evaluated an AI-based diagnostic support system. The vendor provided marketing materials and performance statistics, but when we asked for the model card—training data characteristics, target population specifications, known failure modes—they essentially said that information was proprietary. Our procurement team couldn't determine whether the system was validated for our patient demographics or our specific clinical workflows. We moved forward anyway because we needed something, but we did it partly blind.

Meanwhile, I watched my mother spend three months trying to get her cardiology records transferred when her doctor retired. The portability barriers were real and frustrating. But here's what concerns me: when we talk about removing certification criteria that require developers to document what their tools do and don't do well, we're not really lightening the load on innovation. We're shifting the risk. Hospitals still have to evaluate these systems. Patients still need to trust them. We're just doing it with less information.

The model transparency requirements—the detailed documentation of intended use, target populations, training data composition, and known limitations—these aren't anti-innovation constraints. They're the data that allow procurement teams to make actual decisions instead of guesses. Removing them doesn't reduce burden; it just pushes the burden onto health systems, and they'll respond by either not adopting new tools or adopting them without proper fit-to-purpose assessment.

I've seen the other side of this. A colleague's identity was stolen through a data breach at a clinic that had cut corners on infrastructure to save money. Security requirements exist because the consequences of skipping them are real.

What we need is clarity about which certification criteria actually create unnecessary friction versus which ones serve a real gatekeeping function. If a requirement can be documented differently, faster, or more efficiently without losing essential information, that's a good target for revision. But removing the requirement itself—that hospitals and patients understand what a tool is designed to do—that's a trade-off I'm not comfortable with, even if it does help developers move faster.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: Model card disclosure requirements for clinical AI systems should be strengthened rather than weakened, as transparency about training data, validation populations, and known limitations is essential for clinician trust and patient safety—not regu...
Comment:
Re: RFI on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity

Three years ago my wife enrolled in a diabetes management study we coordinated here at DiMe. When she needed to switch endocrinologists for continuity of care, her previous provider claimed the data transfer would take eight weeks and cost $500. Glucose readings, medication adjustments, the whole clinical record—essentially held hostage by a system that technically belonged to her. That experience sits at the heart of why I'm concerned about the direction of this RFI, particularly around model card disclosures for clinical AI systems.

I understand the developer burden argument. I work with vendors constantly and know the compliance costs are real. But what bothers me is that we're talking about removing transparency requirements for tools that make clinical decisions about patient safety. That's not the same as streamlining interoperability standards. My wife's data portability crisis happened partly because the security and access rules weren't clear enough to be enforced consistently. If we start removing the detailed disclosure requirements for AI model cards now, we're creating the same kind of opacity that trapped her records in the first place.

Model cards aren't regulatory excess. They're documentation. They tell a clinician what the algorithm actually does, what it's been tested on, where it might fail. When an endocrinologist is looking at an AI recommendation for insulin dosing, they need to know whether that model was trained on a population like my wife's or whether the training data skewed toward different demographics. Right now, many vendors treat this as a black box—"trust us, it works." That's not adoption. That's resistance.

I think the question you should be asking isn't which model card requirements to drop, but which ones matter most for patient safety and clinical trust. Keep the ones that force transparency about training data, validation populations, and known limitations. Those are the floor, not the burden. Everything else—the developer workflows, the technical infrastructure—that can probably be streamlined.

The vendors who are serious about clinical adoption actually want clarity. It makes their case to hospitals and practices easier. It's the ones cutting corners that lobby for deregulation.

Paul Nelson
Digital Medicine Society
Missouri

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: Removing transparency requirements for hospital AI tools is dangerous and irresponsible, as healthcare providers need clear information about how AI systems work and their safety record to make informed procurement decisions, just as construction ...
Comment:
Last year my daughter switched insurance and needed her medical records transferred between clinics. It took three months. The offices kept saying their systems didn't talk to each other, and meanwhile she had to repeat expensive lab work because nobody could find anything. I ended up helping pay for tests that should have already been on file. That experience showed me how broken the system is when information doesn't move smoothly between providers.

Now I'm reading about removing transparency requirements for AI tools in hospitals, and it worries me. Look, I work in construction. Before we use any equipment on a job site, we know exactly what it is, how it works, and whether it's been tested for safety. You can't just slap a tool on a building without inspection. Same thing should apply here. When hospitals are deciding whether to use an AI system to help diagnose patients or manage their care, the procurement teams need clear information about how that tool actually works and whether it's reliable for their specific patients and workflows. Removing those transparency requirements means they lose the data they need to make that decision. That's like removing safety inspections before equipment gets approved.

I get that regulations can be burdensome, but transparency isn't burden—it's the foundation for trust. Hospitals need to know what they're putting into their systems. Patients deserve to know too.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: A smaller EHR developer warns that removing AI transparency and certification requirements under the guise of deregulation will unfairly advantage large vendors who can afford to skip compliance costs, while smaller companies like theirs remain lo...
Comment:
Thank you for the opportunity to comment on this RFI. I need to be direct: I'm worried that what's being framed as deregulation is actually going to lock smaller developers like us out of the market while giving the big players a massive free pass.

Last year we invested forty thousand dollars to get our EHR module certified. We did the work—transparency requirements, AI model disclosures, the whole certification process. We believed it was the right investment, the kind of thing that would level the playing field and prove we could meet real standards. Then we watched a startup with half our functionality skip the certification timeline entirely and go live in three months. Turns out they'd found a loophole in the old requirements. We couldn't figure out how they did it, but they did.

Now I'm hearing the agency wants to remove AI model card disclosures and strip back transparency requirements because there's "no published evidence" supporting their impact. That logic bothers me. The fact that we don't have ten years of outcome literature on transparency yet doesn't mean transparency isn't working—it means transparency is still new. Maturity takes time. Robust longitudinal studies don't materialize overnight. Conflating the absence of evidence with evidence of absence is how you throw out something before it actually gets a chance to prove itself.

Here's what keeps me up at night: if you make these requirements optional for developers, who actually drops them? The big vendors who can afford to absorb the compliance cost without blinking. They'll skip the transparency steps, cut their costs, and undercut everyone else. Meanwhile, we stay locked in because our customers—clinics, smaller health systems—they're going to keep demanding accountability from the vendors they choose. We'll be paying for transparency while our competitors aren't. That's not deregulation. That's giving a subsidy to the people who needed it least.

Regarding the second question about revisions to conditions and maintenance of certification: I'd push back on the framing. The issue isn't that certification is too strict. The issue is whether you're going to enforce it evenly or let people cherry-pick which rules apply to them. If you're going to pull back requirements, do it across the board. Don't create a two-tier system where transparency becomes optional for some vendors and mandatory for others. That undoes everything the certification program was supposed to accomplish.

My bigger concern is this: if the standard shifts now, what happens to the forty grand we already spent? Do we get to stop doing what we were asked to do? Do our customers suddenly decide we're too expensive because our competitors found the loophole? We're not opposed to burden reduction. We're opposed to burden reduction that only works for people with enough leverage to negotiate their own rules.

SAFI Clinical Informatics Group LLC

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: While improved data sharing between healthcare providers is necessary, reducing certification standards for medical AI systems is counterproductive unless accompanied by mandatory transparency requirements that allow doctors and patients to unders...
Comment:
Last year when my daughter's pediatrician changed hospital systems, getting her vaccination records took three weeks. Three weeks. The school deadline was coming and I was panicking, calling back and forth between offices, getting transferred around. It made me realize how fragile this whole system is—records should move with patients, not get stuck in silos.

But here's what I'm actually worried about with all this talk of reducing requirements: I don't want faster data sharing if it means hospitals and clinics are using tools nobody understands. When doctors are making decisions about my kid's health using AI systems, I need to know how those systems actually work. Not a marketing pitch from some software company, but real transparency about what the AI is trained on, what its limitations are, where it might get things wrong. I wouldn't buy a car without understanding how the brakes work, and I'm definitely not comfortable with medical AI that's a complete black box.

The vendors pushing to strip out certification standards will tell you it slows things down, but I think the opposite happens. If my pediatrician doesn't trust the system, she won't use it. If patients like me can't see what we're being treated with, we hesitate. That kills adoption faster than any regulation. What actually moves things forward is getting transparency right from the start—model cards, disclosure requirements, that kind of thing. It's not a burden. It's what builds the confidence doctors and patients need to actually use these tools.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: Proactive AI transparency requirements (model card disclosure) in DSI certification would create a level playing field for startups and smaller vendors by establishing one clear baseline standard upfront, whereas waiting for documented harms befor...
Comment:
Deborah Thompson
Health IT Director
Verve, LLC
Kentucky

Last year, we were seriously considering acquiring a health IT startup that had built something genuinely useful—a clinical decision support tool that could have made a real difference for our providers. The founders were solid, the product worked, and the market timing was right. What killed the deal wasn't the technology or the market. It was that they'd spent eighteen months navigating overlapping compliance frameworks just to get one feature certified, when internally we knew it should have taken three months to build. Meanwhile, the vendor we ended up acquiring—a much larger company with teams dedicated to compliance—barely slowed down. They'd already baked in the same safeguards years before any of this was formalized. They absorbed the costs easily. The startup couldn't.

That experience stuck with me, and it's why I'm writing about the AI transparency question in the DSI certification framework.

I get that regulators are rightfully cautious about moving fast. But I keep thinking about what we're actually choosing if we wait for documented failures before requiring AI model card disclosure. In healthcare, that choice has real consequences. Patients could be harmed before we collectively decide transparency is necessary. We'd erode trust in digital health systems at exactly the moment we need to be building it. And from a competitive fairness standpoint, we'd replicate the exact problem I watched unfold: larger vendors would absorb reactive compliance changes without much disruption, while smaller companies and startups would face yet another ad-hoc requirement layered on top of fragmented, evolving expectations.

I'm not arguing for endless regulation. I'm arguing for the opposite, actually. Clear, uniform model card requirements embedded in DSI certification now would create one baseline standard that everyone has to meet upfront. That's predictable. That's fair. That's what lets innovative companies compete on product quality instead of on their ability to navigate overlapping frameworks.

The deregulatory impulse here is sound—reduce unnecessary burden, clear away what doesn't protect patients or interoperability. But stripping away transparency requirements before they're even established isn't reducing burden. It's creating the conditions for more fragmentation, more reactive scrambling, and more uneven playing fields down the road.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The commenter argues that AI model card transparency requirements should be maintained during the evidence-gathering phase, cautioning against deregulation based on the current absence of published outcome data—a methodologically unsound approach ...
Comment:
Having spent two decades analyzing clinical datasets, I've learned that the absence of published evidence is not the same as evidence of absence. This distinction matters enormously when we're discussing AI model card transparency requirements.

Last year, my daughter needed orthopedic surgery and switched hospitals. Her imaging from the first facility sat on a legacy system for three days—the two hospitals' EHRs simply couldn't talk to each other. Her new surgical team couldn't access the studies, so they ordered duplicate MRIs. I watched them do it, knowing exactly what it cost the system and how it frustrated them, but I also understood why those certification standards existed in the first place. Information gaps create clinical risk. They create redundancy. They delay care.

That's what I think about when I consider removing AI transparency requirements. Model cards are functionally similar to interoperability standards—they reduce information asymmetries that can lead to clinical errors and inefficient care. The difference is timing.

These certification criteria for AI transparency are new. Really new. Robust outcome literature doesn't materialize in months. It takes years of real-world implementation, careful measurement, and peer review. Right now we don't have that literature yet, and I know what that looks like in clinical research—it's the normal lag between implementation and evidence accumulation, not evidence that the requirements don't work.

If we deregulate based on the current absence of published outcome data, we're making a methodologically unsound decision. We'll eliminate transparency safeguards before we've had time to actually study whether they improve patient safety and reduce the kind of information gaps that lead to duplicate procedures and care delays.

I understand the desire to reduce developer burden. Certification requirements do impose costs. But the right question isn't whether we have three years of outcome studies yet. The right question is whether we're willing to wait for the evidence before we dismantle protections designed to prevent the same information failures that nearly cost my daughter's surgical team time and created unnecessary imaging.

Keep the transparency requirements in place while we actually study their impact. That's what the data would recommend.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The commenter argues that AI model card disclosure requirements should not be eliminated in proposed deregulation because they provide essential transparency about training data, target populations, and known limitations—information clinicians nee...
Comment:
The past year has given me a lot of time to think about what happens when regulatory frameworks fail to align with the actual work of delivering care and generating the evidence we need to improve it. Last spring, our cardiology department attempted to integrate three EHR platforms across our hospital system. It should have been straightforward—we're talking about basic demographic data, procedure codes, and patient outcomes. Instead, we spent six months negotiating with vendor legal teams just to access data standards that, in any rational system, would be interoperable by design. By the time we had the data extracted and cleaned, the clinical question we wanted to answer had moved on. The moment had closed.

That experience sits behind everything I want to say in this comment, and I think it matters for understanding why the proposed deregulation concerns me more than it excites me.

The American College of Cardiology appreciates the Administration's focus on innovation and competition in health IT. We do. Vendors have legitimate complaints about regulatory burden, and there are inefficiencies in the current certification and standards landscape that slow down product development and market entry. But the notice requests comment on removing detailed AI model card disclosures—the requirement that developers disclose intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and validation processes. On that specific point, I want to push back, and I want to do it from the perspective of someone who has sat in both seats: as a health economist trying to use health IT infrastructure for research, and as a clinician trying to deploy tools responsibly in a high-stakes environment.

The model card requirement isn't regulatory friction. It's foundational transparency.

Here's what I mean. Last year, we evaluated three different AI-based risk stratification tools for our atrial fibrillation screening program. All three were marketed as validated, all three had FDA clearance or were under review, and all three came with very different performance profiles depending on patient demographics. One tool had been trained predominantly on data from patients over 65. Another was built on a cohort that was 78 percent male. A third had been validated in academic medical centers but we had no information about how it would perform in our safety-net clinic population, where comorbidity burden and medication adherence look different. Without the model card requirement—without being forced to disclose those training characteristics, those known limitations, that specificity about target populations—we would have had to reverse-engineer that information ourselves through pilot testing. We did pilot testing anyway, but only because we're large enough and skeptical enough to invest the time.

Most institutions aren't. Most will deploy a tool, assume it works for everyone, and find out six months later that it's missing diagnoses in specific subgroups. That's not innovation. That's risk transfer. And the cost of that risk transfer shows up not in vendor balance sheets but in patient outcomes and in the liability exposure of hospitals that deployed tools without understanding their limitations.

I want to be clear about what I'm not saying. I'm not saying the certification process is well-calibrated. I'm not saying we should require model cards for every minor software update or every incremental improvement to an existing tool. I'm not saying the current ONC Health IT Certification Program is the gold standard. It has real problems. Vendors do spend inordinate amounts of time and money on compliance that doesn't meaningfully improve patient safety. But the solution to bad regulation isn't no regulation; it's better regulation, more narrowly tailored to the actual risks.

The distinction matters because AI in healthcare is genuinely different from traditional software. A charting system has poor UX and you get frustrated. A diagnostic AI tool trained on a dataset that doesn't represent your patient population and you miss diagnoses in vulnerable groups. Those aren't equivalent problems. The FDA recognized this in its 2021 action plan on AI/ML-based software as a medical device. CMS recognized it in the proposed Medicare payment structures for AI-enabled tools. ONC recognized it when it added the model card requirement to the 2024 certification criteria. That recognition was correct.

What concerns me about the deregulatory proposal is that it collapses this distinction. It treats all regulatory requirements as equivalent friction, without discriminating between requirements that address genuine safety issues and requirements that are genuinely redundant. The model card requirement addresses a safety issue. It's asking vendors to disclose information that clinicians and health systems need in order to deploy tools responsibly. That's not burden; that's baseline transparency.

Let me give you a concrete example. In 2023, a major health system deployed an AI-based sepsis detection tool across its ICUs without disclosing that the tool had been trained on a dataset weighted toward older patients with specific comorbidity patterns. Within four months, clinicians noticed the tool was flagging fewer

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The commenter opposes weakening certification requirements for AI systems in healthcare, arguing that detailed model card disclosures—which document how AI systems work, who they were tested on, and known risks—are essential accountability measure...
Comment:
My daughter's pediatrician in Reno switched to a new electronic health system last year, and it was a nightmare. I needed her vaccination records for school enrollment in August, which should have been straightforward—these are records the clinic already had on file. Instead, it took three months to get them pulled up in the new system, and when I finally did, half the dates were wrong. One vaccine was listed as administered in 2019 when it actually happened in 2021. I had to go back and forth with the clinic multiple times to get it corrected, and the whole thing made me nervous about what else might be mixed up in there.

That experience is why I'm concerned about loosening the rules around how these health IT systems are built and certified. I get it—companies need flexibility to innovate and move faster. But from where I sit, the problem isn't that the rules are too strict. The problem is that when systems fail or get implemented poorly, patients like me are the ones who suffer the consequences.

What worries me specifically is the proposal to weaken the certification requirements for AI systems used in healthcare. Right now, companies have to provide detailed information about how their AI models work—what they're designed to do, who they're supposed to work for, what the known risks are, what data was used to train them. This transparency requirement exists for a reason. When a pediatrician's office implements a new system with AI running parts of it, I need to know that someone actually checked whether that system was tested on kids like my daughter, whether it's known to have problems with certain populations, whether there are documented risks.

The certification standards that require these "model cards"—detailed disclosures about AI systems—aren't bureaucratic busywork. They're basic accountability. If something goes wrong, if my daughter's records get mixed up with another patient's, or if an AI system flags something incorrectly, I want to know that the system was properly vetted before it was put into a clinic. Right now, that vetting is part of the certification process.

I understand the argument that reducing certification requirements will speed up innovation and let smaller companies compete. But there's a difference between streamlining paperwork and removing safeguards. Healthcare isn't like other industries. When a software glitch happens in a health system, it doesn't just inconvenience someone—it can directly affect patient safety. My daughter's wrong vaccination dates are a small example, but they illustrate the point. If I hadn't caught it, her school might have thought she wasn't properly immunized. That's not just an administrative error.

What I'm asking is that you keep the detailed disclosure requirements for AI systems used in healthcare. These don't have to be burdensome. They just need to exist. Require companies to document what their AI systems are designed to do, who they were tested on, what problems they've encountered, what the limitations are. This gives healthcare providers and patients actual information to work with. It creates accountability.

I'm not against deregulation across the board. But I think there's a difference between removing unnecessary red tape and removing the transparency measures that actually protect people. The model card requirements are transparent. They're manageable. And they matter when my family's health information is at stake.

I'm asking that you retain the current certification criterion requiring detailed AI model card disclosures for any health IT systems that will be used in clinical settings. Let the system designers innovate on features and functionality. But don't remove the requirement that they explain how their AI systems actually work and what risks they carry. That's not a barrier to innovation—that's just basic accountability, and it's what patients need in order to trust that the systems handling our health information have actually been checked.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The commenter supports reducing regulatory burden on health technology developers but opposes removing AI model card disclosure requirements, arguing that the absence of published evidence of benefit does not justify eliminating transparency prote...
Comment:
RE: Request for Information on Health Technology Certification Criteria and Deregulation

Alliance for Connected Care appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed changes to health technology certification requirements. We represent community organizations across New Mexico and the Southwest working directly with patients and families navigating our fragmented health system.

Last spring, I spent three weeks helping an older woman—a retiree on a fixed income—get her medical records transferred between her cardiologist in Albuquerque and a rural clinic two hours from her home. The delay in sharing her full cardiac history created real uncertainty for her new doctor and added stress at a time when she needed continuity most. That experience taught me how badly we need better interoperability. But I also learned something else: when you're not wealthy, when you don't have time to hire lawyers or consultants, you're vulnerable to whatever happens with your information without your knowledge or meaningful control.

We support reducing unnecessary burden on developers. Interoperability matters. But I'm concerned about removing transparency requirements—specifically AI model card disclosures—before we actually know whether they protect people or not. The rule says transparency standards lack published evidence of benefit. That's true, but I think that's backwards reasoning. These standards are too new to have generated robust outcome studies. Absence of studies is not the same as absence of real-world benefit.

Here's what worries me: vulnerable populations—rural communities, people with limited resources, low-income families—already struggle to get good care and already have fewer ways to advocate if something goes wrong. If we remove AI transparency requirements now, we're essentially asking those folks to be the test subjects while the field figures out whether transparency actually matters. That doesn't seem fair.

Community organizations need model card disclosures so patients and families can understand what they're agreeing to when their health data feeds into AI systems that might affect their care decisions. We need to keep that baseline protection in place. Reducing burden is important, but not before we have evidence that removing these safeguards is actually safe for the people who have the least ability to protect themselves.

Michelle Nelson
Community Organizer, Alliance for Connected Care
Santa Fe, New Mexico

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: A rural health IT vendor argues that while some redundant certification testing should be eliminated, transparency requirements for AI model disclosures must be strengthened rather than weakened, as removing them would unfairly advantage competito...
Comment:
Thank you for the opportunity to respond to this Request for Information on health IT certification and interoperability requirements.

Freedom Home Health Care operates across three rural counties in West Virginia, serving clinics and patient populations that frankly don't have much margin for error. We spent eighteen months and close to $200,000 getting our patient monitoring system certified so we could legally integrate with the clinical workflows these rural providers depend on. I'm not exaggerating when I say we were meticulous—every integration tested, every data element validated, every handoff documented. We did this because rural patients deserve the same safety standards as anyone else, and because we believed that bar existed for good reason.

What troubles me now is watching competitors launch nearly identical solutions in weeks, cutting corners on interoperability validation because the guidance has become so ambiguous. One startup I know about explicitly told their development team to skip AI model card disclosures in their clinical decision support tool. Their logic was straightforward: if the rules keep shifting, why invest in transparency that nobody's going to check? They're undercutting our prices by 40 percent and grabbing market share.

Here's what I want to say clearly: I'm not against reducing unnecessary burden. But there's a difference between cutting red tape and creating a competitive system where vendors who do the work right get punished by vendors who don't. On the certification criteria question—the one about what should actually be removed—I'd point to redundant testing requirements that measure the same interoperability outcome three different ways. That's real waste. But AI model card disclosures? Those shouldn't be on the chopping block.

The agency seems to suggest that transparency requirements lack published evidence of positive impact, and I think that misses something important. These mandates are too new to have generated rigorous clinical literature. You can't run a five-year outcomes study on something that's only been required for two years. That doesn't mean the underlying value isn't there. When a clinician is using an algorithm to flag high-risk patients, they need to understand what that algorithm actually does, what it was trained on, where it might fail. That's not bureaucratic theater. That's accountability. That's patient safety. The fact that we haven't yet published a randomized controlled trial proving it helps doesn't make it less foundational.

If you remove detailed transparency requirements in the name of reducing burden, you've essentially created two markets: one where vendors like us compete on safety and openness, and one where competitors cut corners and still undercut our prices. That's not deregulation that promotes innovation. That's deregulation that rewards corner-cutting.

I'd ask that conditions and maintenance of certification stay tied to meaningful disclosure standards, particularly around algorithmic decision support. Scaling back the redundancy in testing protocols? Yes. Weakening the requirement that developers explain what their tools actually do? That's a different kind of cost reduction—one that gets passed on to rural clinics that can't afford to hire data scientists to reverse-engineer proprietary systems.

Daniel Smith
Healthcare Administrator
Freedom Home Health Care Inc.
Beckley, West Virginia

Comment:
My mother's stroke last year taught me something I wish I hadn't had to learn the hard way. Three weeks of phone calls between her cardiologist, the hospital, and her primary care doctor. Nobody's systems talked to each other. I ended up printing out her medications, her test results, and driving around like a file courier in 2023. It was absurd. So when I hear about making health data easier to access and move between providers, I think yes, we need that. But I also need to know something else.

When a recommendation about my mother's care comes from a doctor, I want to know it came from a person who studied medicine for years and takes responsibility for that decision. If it comes from an AI system, I deserve to know that too. I deserve to know what that system can and can't do, what it's been tested on, where it might fail. That's not a burden on developers—that's the baseline for trust in healthcare. Right now, you can hide a lot behind the word "innovation." You can say removing these rules makes things faster and cheaper. But my mother's care already showed me what happens when systems cut corners for convenience. Somebody ends up managing the mess.

I'm asking you not to weaken the rules that require AI systems to disclose what they are and how they work. Patients have earned the right to know what's deciding their care. That's not negotiable.

Last year when my cardiologist retired, I had to switch to someone across town. My new doctor couldn't pull up any of my previous test results or medication history for three weeks. So I ended up repeating expensive cardiac tests because the two systems just wouldn't communicate with each other. That's the kind of thing that happens when you loosen standards—companies cut corners, patients get hurt, and nobody's accountable.

I hear that loosening up certification requirements might help tech companies move faster and compete more easily. I get that argument. But faster isn't better if it means less oversight on how these systems handle my health data or how the algorithms inside them actually work. What I need to know is: will my information be secure when it moves between providers? How was the software built? What can go wrong, and what happens if it does? Right now, these systems are basically black boxes. Model cards and real transparency requirements aren't just bureaucratic red tape—they're how patients and doctors like mine understand what we're actually relying on.

I'm not against innovation. I'm against the idea that we have to sacrifice transparency and accountability to get it. When you remove certification standards to reduce burden on developers, you're shifting that burden onto patients. We're the ones who repeat tests, who have our data lost in the gaps, who live with the consequences when an algorithm makes a bad call because nobody—not even the doctors—knows how it was designed.

Before cutting certification requirements, I'd ask: which standards can you loosen without compromising patient safety and data security? Because my experience tells me those things matter more than speed.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: While supporting regulatory streamlining to reduce burdensome documentation and audit requirements that disproportionately harm smaller vendors, the commenter argues that mandatory AI model transparency disclosures should remain non-negotiable saf...
Comment:
Thank you for the opportunity to respond to this Request for Information on health IT certification reform. I want to be direct about my perspective here: I've lived on both sides of this issue, and it's made me think hard about what "lighter regulation" should actually mean.

Eighteen months ago, my team at beHuman Corp finished certification for our patient engagement platform. The process nearly destroyed us financially. We hired an outside consultant just to navigate the documentation requirements—that consultant cost more than some of our early employees. I watched competitors with deeper pockets absorb that burden like it was nothing. Smaller vendors either hired consultants or they didn't launch. That's not competition. That's a tax on innovation.

So I understand the impulse behind this deregulatory action. I do. But I'm also watching my daughter's startup prepare to enter this market, and what keeps me up at night is that we might just shuffle the burden around instead of actually reducing it.

Here's what concerns me: if we strip away process requirements—the documentation, the audit trails, the certification busywork—that's genuinely helpful. Those things slow down the small players more than the big ones. But there's a line between reducing burden and reducing safety. And I think that line sits exactly where AI transparency needs to be.

The reason I say this is that AI model disclosures aren't like most certification criteria. They're not paperwork for paperwork's sake. They're prevention. Right now, we're in a window where health AI adoption is accelerating, but we don't yet have documented clinical failures because the systems haven't been in widespread use long enough. Waiting for those failures—waiting to see a patient harmed because a vendor hid the limitations of their algorithm—that's not acceptable. That's ethically indefensible, and it will destroy public trust in healthcare AI faster than anything else.

But here's the thing that nobody seems to notice: mandatory, clear AI model card disclosure actually levels the playing field for smaller vendors. Right now, the large vendors can afford to obscure complexity. They have teams that can make things sound simpler than they are, and their customers are locked in anyway. A startup like my daughter's? She competes on merit. If the certification barrier is "prove what your model does and doesn't do," then transparency becomes her advantage, not her burden. Smaller teams are often more willing to acknowledge limitations because they don't have corporate legal teams second-guessing every word.

The certification burden argument shouldn't apply to safety-critical transparency requirements. We can absolutely streamline the process documentation. We can remove redundant audit trails and consolidate submission formats. But the substantive requirement to disclose what AI systems actually do—their training data limitations, their known failure modes, their performance across different patient populations—that needs to stay non-negotiable. It just needs to be standardized so it's not a different burden for every vendor.

My question for the agency is whether the revision process can distinguish between burden that obscures accountability and burden that streamlines it. Because what I'm worried about is that a one-size-fits-all deregulation will inadvertently protect the vendors who benefit from opacity while making it harder for the innovative smaller players to compete on transparency.

I'd rather see certification criteria streamlined around AI disclosure than eliminated from it.

Title: Comment on CMS-2025-0050
Abstract: The commenter opposes removing AI model card disclosure requirements from health IT certification standards, arguing that transparency about how algorithmic systems work is essential for patient safety, clinical trust, and accountability—and that ...
Organization Name: National Alliance for Care at Home
Comment:
The National Alliance for Care at Home appreciates the opportunity to comment on the proposed deregulatory actions outlined in this notice. Our organization has spent the past decade working with patients, family caregivers, and home-based care providers to ensure that health information flows where it matters most—to the people making day-to-day decisions about their own care or their loved ones' care. That mission has taught us something that might seem obvious but apparently isn't: patients need information they can understand and trust, and the systems delivering that information need to be built on a foundation of transparency and genuine security.

My own experience three years ago with my mother's dementia diagnosis was, frankly, a wake-up call. We couldn't get her records in one place because four different Milwaukee providers said their systems couldn't talk to each other. One clinic asked for a $50 fee to release her digital files. Another told us their "compatibility issues" meant we'd have to request information in person, on paper, every time. But the thing that really scared us—that I still think about—was when one of those providers called us six months after the fact to say they'd had a security incident. They didn't find it themselves. An outside researcher caught it. We never learned exactly what was exposed, and I spent weeks wondering if my mother's cognitive decline was being tracked somewhere she didn't know about, by someone she'd never met.

That experience is why I'm writing this comment, and why I want to be direct about what concerns me in this proposal. I understand the argument that certification requirements are burdensome. I get that health IT vendors face costs and compliance headaches. But the deregulatory action you're considering—particularly the proposal to remove detailed AI model card disclosures from the Data Segmentation for Privacy (DSI) certification criterion—feels like we're solving the wrong problem at the expense of patient safety.

Let me be clear about what I think the rule should do. The current DSI certification criterion requires that certified health IT systems disclose detailed model cards for any artificial intelligence or algorithmic decision-making. Those model cards have to include intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, and validation processes. It's dense, technical stuff. It's not something my mother would read. But it's something her doctor should read before using a system to flag medication interactions, predict her hospitalization risk, or decide which services to recommend. Right now, if you're a clinic in Wisconsin or anywhere else, you can't even know what you're putting into the patient record because the vendors haven't disclosed how their AI systems work.

The proposal to eliminate or water down these disclosures troubles me for reasons that go beyond compliance burden. Three of them stand out.

First, transparency is the only real protection patients have in a healthcare system where they're fundamentally dependent on their providers' judgment. My mother couldn't evaluate whether her clinic's electronic health record system was giving her doctors good information or biased information. She had no way to know. That asymmetry is baked into the patient-provider relationship, and I'm not sure we can fix it entirely. But we can at least require that the tools making decisions about her care are documented in a way that lets her doctor—and ultimately her—know what's happening. When you remove AI model card requirements, you're not just reducing burden on vendors. You're eliminating the mechanism by which clinicians and patients can understand whether the systems they're relying on are trustworthy. You're making that asymmetry deeper.

Second, clinical adoption depends on trust. This is something I hear from home-based care providers all the time. They're skeptical of health IT because they've been burned before—they've implemented systems that didn't work, didn't integrate with other systems, or gave them information they couldn't use. When vendors can't or won't explain how their AI systems work, providers assume the worst. They either don't use the systems at all, or they use them while keeping detailed manual notes on the side, which defeats the whole purpose of digitization. Transparency doesn't burden adoption; in a lot of cases, it enables it. Providers want to know what they're using. Patients want to know what's being used on them.

Third, and maybe most important: accountability means nothing without disclosure. If something goes wrong—if an AI system flags a false medication interaction, or misidentifies a patient's risk category, or systematically performs worse for certain populations—there's no way to trace back to what happened. You can't audit a system you don't understand. You can't identify bias in training data you've never seen. You can't report it. You can't learn from it. What you get instead is the scenario I lived through: a security incident discovered by accident, disclosed late, with no transparency about what happened or
